Among global models, TiDE, a simple neural network architecture, achieves the best accuracy; it also consistently outperforms local models and has lower computational requirements. Large global models are instead much more computationally demanding and less accurate. Among the distribution heads, the Tweedie provides the best estimates of the highest quantiles.
author: Stefano Damato, Nicol\`o Rubattu, Dario Azzimonti, Giorgio Corani

title: A Survey on Evaluating Quality and Trustworthiness in LLM-Generated Data
content:
arXiv:2601.17717v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for generating data across various modalities. By transforming data from a scarce resource into a controllable asset, LLMs mitigate the bottlenecks imposed by the acquisition costs of real-world data for model training, evaluation, and system iteration. However, ensuring the high quality of LLM-generated synthetic data remains a critical challenge. Existing research primarily focuses on generation methodologies, with limited direct attention to the quality of the resulting data. Furthermore, most studies are restricted to single modalities, lacking a unified perspective across different data types. To bridge this gap, we propose the \textbf{LLM Data Auditor framework}. In this framework, we first describe how LLMs are utilized to generate data across six distinct modalities. More importantly, we systematically categorize intrinsic metrics for evaluating synthetic data from two dimensions: quality and trustworthiness. This approach shifts the focus from extrinsic evaluation, which relies on downstream task performance, to the inherent properties of the data itself. Using this evaluation system, we analyze the experimental evaluations of representative generation methods for each modality and identify substantial deficiencies in current evaluation practices. Based on these findings, we offer concrete recommendations for the community to improve the evaluation of data generation. Finally, the framework outlines methodologies for the practical application of synthetic data across different modalities.
author: Kaituo Zhang, Mingzhi Hu, Hoang Anh Duy Le, Fariha Kabir Torsha, Zhimeng Jiang, Minh Khai Bui, Chia-Yuan Chang, Yu-Neng Chuang, Zhen Xiong, Ying Lin, Guanchu Wang, Na Zou

title: A Judge-Aware Ranking Framework for Evaluating Large Language Models without Ground Truth
content:
arXiv:2601.21817v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) on open-ended tasks without ground-truth labels is increasingly done via the LLM-as-a-judge paradigm. A critical but under-modeled issue is that judge LLMs differ substantially in reliability; treating all judges equally can yield biased leaderboards and misleading uncertainty estimates. More data can make evaluation more confidently wrong under misspecified aggregation. We propose a judge-aware ranking framework that extends the Bradley-Terry-Luce model by introducing judge-specific discrimination parameters, jointly estimating latent model quality and judge reliability from pairwise comparisons without reference labels. We establish identifiability up to natural normalizations and prove consistency and asymptotic normality of the maximum likelihood estimator, enabling confidence intervals for score differences and rank comparisons. Across multiple public benchmarks and a newly collected dataset, our method improves agreement with human preferences, achieves higher data efficiency than unweighted baselines, and produces calibrated uncertainty quantification for LLM rankings.
author: Mingyuan Xu, Xinzi Tan, Jiawei Wu, Doudou Zhou

title: MentisOculi: Revealing the Limits of Reasoning with Mental Imagery
content:
arXiv:2602.02465v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Frontier models are transitioning from multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that merely ingest visual information to unified multimodal models (UMMs) capable of native interleaved generation. This shift has sparked interest in using intermediate visualizations as a reasoning aid, akin to human mental imagery. Central to this idea is the ability to form, maintain, and manipulate visual representations in a goal-oriented manner. To evaluate and probe this capability, we develop MentisOculi, a procedural, stratified suite of multi-step reasoning problems amenable to visual solution, tuned to challenge frontier models. Evaluating visual strategies ranging from latent tokens to explicit generated imagery, we find they generally fail to improve performance. Analysis of UMMs specifically exposes a critical limitation: While they possess the textual reasoning capacity to solve a task and can sometimes generate correct visuals, they suffer from compounding generation errors and fail to leverage even ground-truth visualizations. Our findings suggest that despite their inherent appeal, visual thoughts do not yet benefit model reasoning. MentisOculi establishes the necessary foundation to analyze and close this gap across diverse model families.
author: Jana Zeller, Thadd\"aus Wiedemer, Fanfei Li, Thomas Klein, Prasanna Mayilvahanan, Matthias Bethge, Felix Wichmann, Ryan Cotterell, Wieland Brendel

title: On the Optimal Reasoning Length for RL-Trained Language Models
content:
arXiv:2602.09591v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Reinforcement learning substantially improves reasoning in large language models, but it also tends to lengthen chain-of-thought outputs and increase computational cost. Although length-control methods have been proposed, the length-accuracy relationship they induce remains unclear. We train policies with several length-control methods on multiple base models in a controlled setup and find that, across both mathematical reasoning and code generation, accuracy is non-monotonic in output length, peaking at an intermediate value. Mode accuracy, however, continues to improve with length even in settings where sample accuracy plateaus or declines, indicating that the non-monotonic length-accuracy relationship is driven by dispersion around an increasingly correct center.
author: Daisuke Nohara, Taishi Nakamura, Rio Yokota

title: SoftMatcha 2: A Fast and Soft Pattern Matcher for Trillion-Scale Corpora
content:
arXiv:2602.10908v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We present SoftMatcha 2, an ultra-fast and flexible search algorithm that enables search over trillion-scale natural language corpora in under 0.3 seconds while allowing semantic variations in the form of substitution, insertion, and deletion. Our approach employs string matching based on suffix arrays that scales well with corpus size, and represents words as vectors, which underpin its semantic flexibility. To mitigate the combinatorial explosion induced by the semantic relaxation of queries, our method is built on two key algorithmic ideas: dynamic corpus-aware pruning and fast exact lookup enabled by a disk-aware design. We theoretically analyze the efficiency of the proposed method, indicating that it can mitigate exponential growth in the search space. Empirically, on FineWeb-Edu (Lozhkov et al., 2024) (1.4T tokens), it attains substantially lower search latency than existing methods: infini-gram (Liu et al., 2024), infini-gram mini (Xu et al., 2025), and SoftMatcha (Deguchi et al., 2025). As a practical application, our method uncovers benchmark contamination in training corpora that existing approaches miss, and it also benefits information retrieval and paraphrase detection. We also provide an online demo of fast, soft search across corpora in seven languages.
author: Masataka Yoneda, Yusuke Matsushita, Go Kamoda, Kohei Suenaga, Takuya Akiba, Masaki Waga, Sho Yokoi

title: Human-Guided Agentic AI for Multimodal Clinical Prediction: Lessons from the AgentDS Healthcare Benchmark
content:
arXiv:2602.19502v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Agentic AI systems are increasingly capable of autonomous data science workflows, yet clinical prediction tasks demand domain expertise that purely automated approaches struggle to provide. We investigate how human guidance of agentic AI can improve multimodal clinical prediction, presenting our approach to all three AgentDS Healthcare benchmark challenges: 30-day hospital readmission prediction (Macro-F1 = 0.8986), emergency department cost forecasting (MAE = $465.13), and discharge readiness assessment (Macro-F1 = 0.7939). Across these tasks, human analysts directed the agentic workflow at key decision points, multimodal feature engineering from clinical notes, scanned PDF billing receipts, and time-series vital signs; task-appropriate model selection; and clinically informed validation strategies. Our approach ranked 5th overall in the healthcare domain, with a 3rd-place finish on the discharge readiness task. Ablation studies reveal that human-guided decisions compounded to a cumulative gain of +0.065 F1 over automated baselines, with multimodal feature extraction contributing the largest single improvement (+0.041 F1). We distill three generalizable lessons: (1) domain-informed feature engineering at each pipeline stage yields compounding gains that outperform extensive automated search; (2) multimodal data integration requires task-specific human judgment that no single extraction strategy generalizes across clinical text, PDFs, and time-series; and (3) deliberate ensemble diversity with clinically motivated model configurations outperforms random hyperparameter search. These findings offer practical guidance for teams deploying agentic AI in healthcare settings where interpretability, reproducibility, and clinical validity are essential.
author: Lalitha Pranathi Pulavarthy, Raajitha Muthyala, Aravind V Kuruvikkattil, Zhenan Yin, Rashmita Kudamala, Saptarshi Purkayastha

title: Neural ensemble Kalman filter: Data assimilation for compressible flows with shocks
content:
arXiv:2602.23461v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Data assimilation (DA) for compressible flows with shocks is challenging because many classical DA methods generate spurious oscillations and nonphysical features near uncertain shocks. We focus here on the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF). We show that the poor performance of the EnKF may be attributed to the bimodal forecast distribution that can arise in the vicinity of an uncertain shock location; this violates the assumptions underpinning the EnKF, which assume a forecast which is close to Gaussian. To address this issue we introduce the new neural EnKF. The basic idea is to systematically embed neural function approximations within ensemble DA by mapping the forecast ensemble of shocked flows to the parameter space (weights and biases) of a deep neural network (NN) and to subsequently perform DA in that space. The nonlinear mapping encodes sharp and smooth flow features in an ensemble of NN parameters. Neural EnKF updates are therefore well-behaved only if the NN parameters vary smoothly within the neural representation of the forecast ensemble. We show that such a smooth variation of network parameters can be enforced via physics-informed transfer learning, and demonstrate that in so-doing the neural EnKF avoids the spurious oscillations and nonphysical features that plague the EnKF. The applicability of the neural EnKF is demonstrated through a series of systematic numerical experiments with the inviscid Burgers' equation, the Sod shock tube, and a two-dimensional blast wave.
author: Xu-Hui Zhou, Lorenzo Beronilla, Michael K. Sleeman, Hangchuan Hu, Matthias Morzfeld, Andrew M. Stuart, Tamer A. Zaki

title: On Regret Bounds of Thompson Sampling for Bayesian Optimization
content:
arXiv:2603.09276v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We study a widely used Bayesian optimization method, Gaussian process Thompson sampling (GP-TS), under the assumption that the objective function is a sample path from a GP. Compared with the GP upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) with established high-probability and expected regret bounds, most analyses of GP-TS have been limited to expected regret. Moreover, whether the recent analyses of GP-UCB for the lenient regret and the improved cumulative regret upper bound can be applied to GP-TS remains unclear. To fill these gaps, this paper shows several regret bounds: (i) a regret lower bound for GP-TS, which implies that GP-TS suffers from a polynomial dependence on $1/\delta$ with probability $\delta$, (ii) an upper bound of the second moment of cumulative regret, which directly suggests an improved regret upper bound on $\delta$, (iii) expected lenient regret upper bounds, and (iv) an improved cumulative regret upper bound on the time horizon $T$. Along the way, we provide several useful lemmas, including a relaxation of the necessary condition from recent analysis to obtain improved regret upper bounds on $T$.
author: Shion Takeno, Shogo Iwazaki

title: A theory of learning data statistics in diffusion models, from easy to hard
content:
arXiv:2603.12901v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: While diffusion models have emerged as a powerful class of generative models, their learning dynamics remain poorly understood. We address this issue first by empirically showing that standard diffusion models trained on natural images exhibit a distributional simplicity bias, learning simple, pair-wise input statistics before specializing to higher-order correlations. We reproduce this behaviour in simple denoisers trained on a minimal data model, the mixed cumulant model, where we precisely control both pair-wise and higher-order correlations of the inputs. We identify a scalar invariant of the model that governs the sample complexity of learning pair-wise and higher-order correlations that we call the diffusion information exponent, in analogy to related invariants in different learning paradigms. Using this invariant, we prove that the denoiser learns simple, pair-wise statistics of the inputs at linear sample complexity, while more complex higher-order statistics, such as the fourth cumulant, require at least cubic sample complexity. We also prove that the sample complexity of learning the fourth cumulant is linear if pair-wise and higher-order statistics share a correlated latent structure. Our work describes a key mechanism for how diffusion models can learn distributions of increasing complexity.
author: Lorenzo Bardone, Claudia Merger, Sebastian Goldt

title: A Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion Instrument for Measuring Regional Human Mobility: The Distributed Human Data Engine (DHDE)
content:
arXiv:2603.21639v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Accurately estimating human mobility in peripheral regional economies presents a fundamental measurement challenge: physical ground-truth sensors are sparse, behavioral intent signals are heterogeneous, and environmental friction introduces systematic bias into demand inference. We introduce the Distributed Human Data Engine (DHDE), a multi-modal sensor fusion architecture that addresses this challenge by integrating physical instrumentation (Edge-AI cameras), digital intent signals (route search impression metrics), behavioral records (90,350 spending records, 97,719 standardized survey responses), and meteorological data across four geographically distributed nodes in Fukui, Japan. The primary measurement-science contribution is the design, deployment, and cross-node validation of the DHDE as a sparse-sensor compensation instrument: a heterogeneous sensor fusion architecture that anchors non-stationary digital intent signals to concurrent physical ground-truth counts, correcting for systematic bias introduced by meteorological planning friction. The instrument is implemented as an ensemble inference pipeline (Random Forest and Ordinary Least Squares with Newey-West robust inference), calibrated across 397 daily observations and validated by chronological holdout replication across four geographically distinct node types. The primary OLS specification achieved an in-sample explanatory power of R2 = 0.810 and a chronological out-of-sample predictive performance of R2 = 0.683. Results identify an Under-Vibrancy Paradox where macro-regional visitor satisfaction correlates positively with crowd density (Spearman rank correlation rs = +0.150, p = 0.002). We estimate an annual proxy gap of 865,917 intent-implied visits, corresponding to JPY 11.96 billion (USD 72.6 million) in foregone revenue.
author: Amil Khanzada, Takuji Takemoto

title: Deep Learning of Solver-Aware Turbulence Closures from Nudged LES Dynamics
content:
arXiv:2604.23874v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: The differentiable physics paradigm may be leveraged as an a-posteriori approach for discovering turbulence closure models by embedding a neural network parameterization directly inside the solver and optimizing it given potentially sparse target data. This addresses a key limitation of a-priori learning where direct numerical simulation (DNS) data is used to approximate the subgrid stress with the assumption of a low-pass filter. Closures trained in this a-priori manner frequently lead to unstable deployments due to the mismatch between the assumed filter and the effect of numerical discretizations and coarse-graining. In comparison, while typically stable during deployment, a-posteriori learning incurs high computational costs due to the need to backpropagate through a large eddy simulation (LES) solver. Furthermore, a-posteriori methods are challenging to apply broadly since they require significant modification of existing solvers. Finally, both approaches are limited when generalization is desired across different numerical schemes with their implicit filtering characteristics. In this work, we present a deep-learning approach for turbulence closure modeling built on the continuous data assimilation framework. Our approach enables the a-priori training of closures using sparsely observed DNS data without modifying or differentiating through the LES solver, while preserving stability during deployment for the recovery of invariant statistics. We focus on the model's ability to adapt to different discretizations by explicitly conditioning it on the numerical scheme. We use two- and three-dimensional canonical cases to test our framework and show that the learned correction systematically tracks the discretization error of the coarse solver.
author: Ashwin Suriyanarayanan, Dibyajyoti Chakraborty, Romit Maulik

title: FitText: Evolving Agent Tool Ecologies via Memetic Retrieval
content:
arXiv:2605.02411v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: A semantic gap separates how users describe tasks from how tools are documented. As API ecosystems scale to tens of thousands of endpoints, static retrieval from the initial query alone cannot bridge this gap: the agent's understanding of what it needs evolves during execution, but its tool set does not. We identify this retrieval interface, not planning, as the binding constraint on end-to-end agent performance, and introduce FitText, a training-free framework that makes retrieval dynamic by embedding it directly in the agent's reasoning loop. FitText treats retrieval as test-time evolution of hypotheses: the agent generates natural-language pseudo-tool descriptions (revisable beliefs about the tool it needs), refines them iteratively using retrieval feedback, and explores diverse alternatives through stochastic generation. Memetic Retrieval adds evolutionary selection pressure over candidate descriptions, guided by a tool memory that avoids redundant search. On ToolRet (three domains), FitText's reformulation strategies improve NDCG@5 by 2.7 to 10.6 points over static query retrieval across all base models; on StableToolBench (16,464 APIs) with GPT-5.4-mini, Memetic reaches an 84.3% pooled pass rate, a 26.7-point absolute gain over static query retrieval.
author: Kyle Zheng, Han Zhang, Renliang Sun, Chenchen Ye, Wei Wang

title: CredibleDFGO: Differentiable Factor Graph Optimization with Credibility Supervision
content:
arXiv:2605.06100v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Global navigation satellite system (GNSS) positioning is widely used for urban navigation, but the covariance reported by the GNSS solver is often unreliable in urban canyons. Existing differentiable factor graph optimization (DFGO) methods learn measurement weighting through the solver, but they still use position-only objectives. As a result, the position estimate may improve while the reported covariance remains too small, too large, or incorrectly oriented. We propose CredibleDFGO (CDFGO), a differentiable GNSS factor graph framework that makes covariance credibility an explicit training target. A Weighting Generation Network (WGN) predicts per-satellite reliability weights, and a differentiable Gauss-Newton solver maps these weights to a position estimate and a Hessian-derived posterior covariance. We use proper scoring rules to supervise the East-North predictive distribution end to end. We study negative log-likelihood (NLL), the energy score (ES), and their combination. Results on three UrbanNav test scenes show consistent gains in covariance credibility. Positioning accuracy also improves on the medium-urban and harsh-urban scenes; on the deep-urban scene, both the mean horizontal error and the 95th-percentile error improve. On the harsh-urban Mong Kok (MK) scene, CDFGO-Combined reduces the mean horizontal error from 13.77 m to 11.68 m, reduces NLL from 40.63 to 6.59, and reduces ES from 12.31 to 9.05 relative to DFGO (MAE). Case studies link the MK improvement to better axis-wise consistency, more credible local covariance ellipses, and satellite-level reweighting.
author: Liang Qian, Penggao Yan, Penghui Xu, Li-Ta Hsu

title: A Resilient Solution for Sewer Overflow Monitoring across Cloud and Edge
content:
arXiv:2605.10592v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Aging combined sewer systems in many historical cities are increasingly stressed by extreme rainfall events, which can trigger combined sewer overflows (CSO) with significant environmental and public health impacts. Forecasting the filling dynamics of overflow basins is critical for anticipating capacity exceedance and enabling timely preventive actions for CSO. We present a web-based demonstrator that integrates Deep Learning forecasting methods in both cloud and edge settings into an interactive monitoring dashboard for overflow monitoring, resilient to network outages. A video showcase is available online (https://cloud.bht-berlin.de/index.php/s/b9xt4T3SdiLBiFZ).
author: Vipin Singh, Tianheng Ling, Peter Ghaly, Felix Grimmeisen, Gregor Schiele, Felix Biessmann

title: Right Predictions, Misleading Explanations: On the Vulnerability of Vision-Language Model Explanations
content:
arXiv:2605.16651v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Explanation mechanisms are increasingly used to support transparency and trust in vision-language models (VLMs), particularly in settings where model decisions require human oversight. However, the robustness of these explanations remains insufficiently understood. In this work, we investigate whether explanation heatmaps in VLMs, particularly CLIP-based models, faithfully reflect model reasoning under adversarial conditions. We show that explanation maps can be systematically manipulated while preserving the model's original prediction, revealing a disconnect between predictive behavior and explanation faithfulness. To study this vulnerability, we introduce X-Shift, a novel grey-box attack that perturbs patch-level visual representations to redirect explanation heatmaps toward semantically irrelevant regions without altering the predicted output. Unlike conventional adversarial attacks that aim to induce misclassification, X-Shift specifically targets the integrity of the explanation process itself. The attack operates without modifying model parameters and generalizes across multiple CLIP architectures and explanation methods. We evaluate the proposed approach on ImageNet-1k, MS-COCO, and Flickr30K, demonstrating consistent degradation in explanation alignment under imperceptible perturbations while maintaining prediction stability. Furthermore, standard prediction-oriented adversarial attacks fail to reproduce the same explanation-shifting behavior even under substantially larger perturbation budgets. Our findings highlight a fundamental limitation of current explanation mechanisms in VLMs and raise concerns about their use as reliable indicators of model trustworthiness in high-impact applications.
author: Narges Babadi, Hadis Karimipour

title: Conformal Risk-Averse Decision Making with Action Conditional Guarantee
content:
arXiv:2606.05551v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Reliable decision making pipelines powered by machine learning models require uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods that come with explicit safety guarantees. Conformal prediction provides such UQ by wrapping ML predictions into prediction sets, and recent work by Kiyani et al. (2025b) established that these sets can be translated into optimal risk-averse decision policies -- yet only inheriting marginal safety guarantees. We generalize and strengthen their results by (i) introducing action-conditional conformal prediction, which yields safety guarantees conditioned explicitly on each action taken by the decision maker, (ii) showing that action-conditional prediction sets serve as a proxy for the feasible decision space for risk-averse decision makers aiming to optimize action-conditional value-at-risk, and (iii) proposing a principled finite-sample algorithm based on pinball-loss minimization, connecting the framework of Gibbs et al. (2025) to action-conditional guarantees. Experiments on two real-world datasets confirm that our approach significantly improves action-conditional performance over conformal baselines.
author: Zihan Zhu, Shayan Kiyani, George Pappas, Hamed Hassani

title: Knowledge Manifold: A Riemannian Geometric Framework for Semantic Mapping and Geodesic Analysis of Scientific Literature
content:
arXiv:2606.05907v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We present the knowledge manifold: a Riemannian geometric space in which a corpus of documents is arranged according to semantic positional relationships derived from character n-gram TF-IDF representations. The framework proceeds in five tightly coupled stages. First, each document is converted to a character-level n-gram TF-IDF vector (4-7 grams, up to 250,000 features, L2-normalized) and embedded in a two-dimensional knowledge map via constrained stress minimization with repulsion, variance, and centering regularizers. Second, knowledge at an arbitrary query point is estimated through Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) interpolation using a cubic-spline kernel, yielding an interpolated TF-IDF feature vector that can be linguistically characterized. Third, directional knowledge gradients at 0, 45, and 90 degrees are computed from the SPH interpolation map, and pairwise directional similarity is quantified via inner product and cosine similarity. Fourth, a Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) model, with a Constant x RBF + White kernel fitted on a 10-dimensional SVD projection, provides a Bayesian posterior mean, uncertainty estimate, and per-document contribution rate at the query point. Fifth, geodesics in the knowledge space are obtained by minimizing a discrete Riemannian path energy derived from the SPH-induced metric tensor, using L-BFGS-B with seven deterministic initial-path candidates. We apply the formulation to a corpus of 20 papers in fiber-reinforced composite materials and aerospace structural mechanics, showing that the semantic map recovers meaningful research clusters, geodesic paths reveal natural conceptual bridges between distant topics, and SPH/GPR interpolation enables the generation of virtual knowledge: hypothetical paper abstracts describing unstudied but geometrically predicted research directions.
author: Tomonaga Okabe, Kazuhiko Komatsu

title: Evolving Agents in the Dark: Retrospective Harness Optimization via Self-Preference
content:
arXiv:2606.05922v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: AI agents rely on a harness of skills, tools, and workflows to solve complex problems. Continually improving this harness is essential for adapting to new tasks. However, existing optimization methods typically require ground-truth validation sets, yet such labeled data is difficult to acquire in practical deployment settings. To address this problem, we introduce Retrospective Harness Optimization (RHO), a self-supervised method that optimizes the agent harness using only past trajectories. Specifically, RHO selects a diverse coreset of challenging tasks from past trajectories and re-solves them in parallel. The agent analyzes these rollouts using self-validation and self-consistency, then generates candidate harness updates and selects the most effective one by its own pairwise self-preference. We evaluate RHO across three diverse domains, spanning software engineering, technical work, and knowledge work. Notably, a single optimization round improves the pass rate on SWE-Bench Pro from 59% to 78% without any external grading. Furthermore, our analysis demonstrates that RHO effectively targets prior failure modes. As a result, the optimized harness alters the agent's behavior patterns and sustains higher accuracy during long-horizon sessions.
author: Wenbo Pan, Shujie Liu, Chin-Yew Lin, Jingying Zeng, Xianfeng Tang, Xiangyang Zhou, Yan Lu, Xiaohua Jia

title: An Improved Semi-Empirical Model of Short Wind Waves in Application to Microwave Radar Sounding of Marine Slicks
content: A semi-empirical model of the short wind wave spectrum in the cm-wavelength range is developed based on data from experiments on remote sensing of organic film slicks on the sea surface using X-/C-/S-band radar operating at two co-polarizations at moderate incidence angles. The variability of the Bragg component of the radar return from the contaminated sea surface was analyzed, and the degree of wave suppression (contrast) due to film was obtained for crosswind and along-wind directions. The “crosswind slick contrasts” were compared with theoretical ones obtained from the wave energy balance equation with an additional source responsible for the generation of short gravity-capillary waves (GCWs). The source role is significant at low wind when direct wind forcing is ineffective. An expression for the additional source was obtained in the form of a power function of the wind friction velocity. Comparison between the measured “upwind slick contrasts” and those calculated using the developed model showed that the additional source can be considered isotropic. At moderate wind, this source can be neglected for upwind wave excitation on clean water, whereas in film slicks its contribution is significant or even dominant. A qualitative difference between the “crosswind contrasts” for different films is revealed: at low film elasticity (less than about 10 mN/m) the contrast increases with wavenumber, while at higher elasticity it decreases, which is consistent with experiment at moderate wind.
author: Irina Sergievskaya, Stanislav Ermakov

title: TSC-DETR: DETR With Transformer-Semantics to CNN Guidance for Small Object Detection
content: Object detection models based on the Detection Transformer (DETR) architecture suffer from a performance bottleneck due to its attention mechanism’s bias toward high-level semantic information, resulting in insufficient low-level detail feature extraction. Particularly in small object detection, existing DETR models suffer from significant deficiencies in detail representation due to the limited number of pixels in the target region. To address this issue, we propose the Transformer-semantics to CNN guidance DETR (TSC-DETR), which leverages high-level semantic guidance and a CNN to enhance low-level edge feature extraction for small object detection. First, we introduce a method to enhance the encoder’s dynamic multiscale perception capabilities to compensate for the linear attention mechanism’s limitations in describing edge detail. Second, we utilize a high-level semantic prior prediction guidance map generated by the Transformer encoder to guide the convolutional network to focus more on positive sample regions. Also, to address the problem of small objects being missed due to the “flooding effect” of high-level semantics in negative sample regions, we design a prediction guidance map specifically for negative sample regions to improve the model’s ability to recognize small objects. Furthermore, we constructed a multiscale, multigranularity detail perception module. This module relies on high-level semantics to focus on positive and negative regions, fusing multilevel information to enhance the expressive power of low-level features in depicting the boundaries and textures of small objects. TSC-DETR outperforms previous CNN-based and DETR-based methods, achieving an average precision (AP) of 31.5% on AI-TOD and 36.7% on Visdrone2019. The project page is available at: https://github.com/AuroraLove/tsc-detr
author: Zheng Yu, Li Guo, Hongyuan Huo, Qing Xu, Fang Ren, Qifan Cui

title: Deep Reinforcement Control for Precipitation Prediction and Cloud-Seeding Decisions in Morocco and the Iberian Peninsula
content: We present an operational framework designed to convert hourly European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts Reanalysis Version 5 (ERA5) reanalysis data into informed cloud-seeding decisions over the Moroccan–Iberian region. Seven machine learning and deep learning models were compared across three forecast horizons ( $H{\,}{\,}{=}{\,}{\,}{+}1$  h, +3 h, and +6 h). The predictor set combines atmospheric variables, short-term temporal memory, neighboring spatial information, and physically based indicators related to moisture transport and circulation structure. Among the tested approaches, the enhanced mixture-of-experts (MoE) model showed the most robust behavior for short-range forecasting at the operational horizon  $H{\,}{\,}{=}{\,}{\,}{+}1$  h and was, therefore, retained for the decision stage. In the independent test period, the model reached  $R^{2}{\,}={\,}0.6495$ ,  $\mathrm {KGE}{\,}={\,}0.6013$ , and  $\mathrm {{F}1}{\,}={\,}0.8109$ . The forecast outputs feed into a two-stage eligibility filter that combines rainfall probability with atmospheric suitability, narrowing the candidate windows to those where seeding is physically plausible. Eligible cases are then passed to a reinforcement learning controller, which derives continuous seeding actions from the prevailing atmospheric state under physically realistic constraints. We further assessed the framework across years with contrasting hydroclimatic regimes, including two comparatively dry years (2022 and 2024) alongside a wetter year (2026), and observed consistent behavior from both the forecasting and decision components. The seeding maps exhibit a coherent spatial structure, with intervention potential concentrated along the northern Moroccan Atlantic coast and the western Iberian margin, while the drier southern sectors remain largely inactive. Taken together, this work integrates rainfall forecasting and operational decision support within a unified framework, offering a scalable and physically interpretable foundation for weather-modification strategies in regions marked by strong climatic variability.
author: Imad Katiba, Hanae Belmajdoub, Khalid Minaoui

title: Emission-Intensity-Driven Evolution of Black Carbon Microphysics in Wildfire Smoke Revealed by Multiwavelength Remote Sensing
content: Wildfire activity has intensified globally, yet the microphysical properties of black carbon (BC) in smoke plumes remain poorly constrained in remote sensing retrievals, limiting accurate assessments of aerosol climate forcing. This study develops an integrated framework combining AERONET measurements with satellite-based fire emission data to characterize BC mixing states under varying combustion intensities. Using a core–shell Mie model jointly constrained by multiwavelength single-scattering albedo (SSA) (440–1020 nm) and absorption Ångström exponent (AAE) calculated specifically from 675 to 1020 nm to minimize brown carbon (BrC) interference, we retrieve BC core size, shell thickness, and shell-to-core ratio across four emission levels. Results reveal systematic variations with fire intensity: high-intensity combustion produces bimodal SSA distributions (peaks at 0.85 and 0.90) and narrows BC core size distributions (155–190 nm for C emission level 4 versus 140–200 nm for C emission level 1). Joint AAE–SSA constraints yield shell-to-core ratios of 2.9–3.8, significantly higher than SSA-only retrievals and consistent with field measurements. Regional analysis demonstrates that humidity exerts stronger control over coating thickness than emission intensity. Sensitivity tests excluding 440-nm SSA produce nonphysical solutions under low-intensity conditions, confirming the value of short-wavelength constraints. This empirically grounded framework advances BC microphysical characterization from routine optical measurements, reducing key uncertainties for aerosol-climate interaction assessments.
author: Jie Luo, Yangyang Ma, Hongjin Li, Congcong Li, Xudong Dong, Xu Yang, Pei Li, Peng Zhou, Jun Zhao

title: Improved Modeling of Observation Error, Quality Control, and Bias Correction With Cloud Effects for Assimilating All-Sky FY-3G MWRI-RM Radiances
content: The microwave radiation imager for rainfall mission (MWRI-RM) is the core payload onboard China’s Fengyun-3G (FY-3G) satellite. As the country’s first low-inclination-orbit precipitation measurement satellite, FY-3G achieves integrated microwave imaging and vertical temperature-humidity profiling. The instrument adopts an innovative dual-row detection layout under limited payload constraints, enabling convective-scale microwave observation of precipitation weather systems. Differences between the dual rows in terms of observation angles, spatial resolution, and coverage trajectories pose challenges to the all-sky assimilation of its full-channel observations. This study proposes integrating adaptive cloud effects, which quantify the cloud impacts on microwave radiances, into the MWRI-RM observation error, quality control (QC), and bias correction models to systematically account for uncertainty caused by clouds to better utilize microwave observations in all-sky data assimilation: 1) for all channels distributed at 18.7–36.5 GHz, 50.3–53.75 GHz, 118 GHz, and 183 GHz respectively, channel-dependent cloud effect models and corresponding dynamic observation error models based on polarization difference and statistical characteristics of all-sky observation departures are established to address the dual-row problem; 2) to better eliminate nonlinear samples and improve the quality of observations in cloudy regions, a cloud-dependent QC model for all-sky microwave radiances is developed with the established cloud effects and dynamic observation error models; 3) to better reduce the bias of all-sky microwave radiances caused by clouds, the channel-dependent cloud effect models are also introduced as cloud predictors for bias correction; and 4) finally, the convection-permitting assimilation of all-sky FY-3G microwave radiances was conducted for multiple rainfall cases with the improved modeling of cloud-dependent observation error, QC, and bias correction improved using cloud effects, and the results show the rainfall forecast can be well improved. The combined assimilation of radiances at different bands outperforms any single band, demonstrating the synergistic advantage of the multiband detection of MWRI-RM. This study provides a reference for the better utilization of all-sky microwave observations from similar instruments to be developed in the future, and demonstrates the value of convection-permitting assimilation of microwave radiances onboard precipitation measurement satellites for improving precipitation prediction.
author: Yuanbing Wang, Yaxin Deng, Yaodeng Chen, Jian Shang, Wanli Zhang, Deming Meng

title: Weakly Paired Remote Sensing Change Captioning With Large Multimodal Model
content: Automatically generating textual descriptions of changes between bi-temporal remote sensing images remains challenging due to the labor-intensive and time-consuming process of obtaining fully aligned image–caption paired data. To address this limitation, we propose a novel weakly paired learning framework that harnesses the powerful visual and language capabilities of large multimodal models (LMMs) for remote sensing change captioning. Unlike conventional methods that rely on precisely annotated image-caption pairs, our approach effectively utilizes weakly paired tags that encapsulate changed objects and their corresponding actions for each image. The proposed framework is built upon an LMM architecture and operates synergistically across two distinct visual-language phases via parameter-efficient adaptation. In the visual change perception phase, we characterize spatiotemporal variations by identifying cross-temporal object changes and their associated actions through tag recognition. Subsequently, in the language interpretation phase, we leverage the pretrained LMM to convert these visual change tags into coherent and semantically rich textual descriptions, exploiting the linguistic knowledge embedded in the LMM. To further enhance the alignment of LMM-generated captions with human annotations, we introduce a train-time expansion strategy, which refines alignment by rewriting synonymously expressed captions back into their original phrasing. This strategy improves alignment without incurring additional inference costs. As a plug-and-play method, our framework is compatible with various LMMs and achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art fully supervised approaches, demonstrating its effectiveness in remote sensing change captioning. The code will be available at https://github.com/mrazhou/WeCap
author: Qing Zhou, Junzheng Wu, Yuyu Jia, Shihao Zhou, Junyu Gao, Weiping Ni, Qi Wang

title: Generalized Bayesian Linearized AVO Inversion Using Box-Cox Transformation
content: A generalized Bayesian linearized seismic inversion framework is presented to extend the classical Bayesian linearized inversion by assuming non-Gaussian prior distributions for elastic properties through a parametric normalizing transformation applied directly to the elastic variables. The approach applies a monotonic invertible power function, namely the Box–Cox transformation, to map non-Gaussian elastic variables into a Gaussian space, where computationally efficient Bayesian linearized inversion is performed using linearized amplitude-versus-offset (AVO) models. A low-frequency background trend is incorporated to mitigate the band-limited nature of seismic data. Posterior mean and covariance in the Gaussian space are obtained analytically and subsequently mapped back to the physical domain, yielding posterior distributions that capture realistic uncertainty and prior statistical behavior. The Box–Cox transformation implicitly defines a flexible parametric family for the elastic properties, referred to as the Box–Cox normal distribution, whose shape is controlled by a single parameter  $\lambda $  estimated from measured data by minimizing the skewness of the transformed variable. This family encompasses the lognormal distribution and both right- and left-skewed distributions. The methodology is demonstrated on synthetic and field seismic datasets to estimate the posterior distributions of P-wave velocity, S-wave velocity, and density conditioned on the seismic observations, and to infer the most likely elastic model of the reservoir.
author: Dario Grana, Leandro Passos de Figueiredo, Fernando Luis Bordignon, Rodrigo Exterkoetter, Mauro Roisenberg, Bruno Barbosa Rodrigues

title: Dual-Branch Single-Image Self-Supervised Denoising Network Based on Laplacian Nonlocal Self-Similarity Sampling
content: Optical remote sensing images are widely used in numerous fields, and high-quality images generally play a critical role in downstream tasks. In recent years, self-supervised image denoising methods based on deep learning have achieved significant progress. However, existing sampling strategies in self-supervised approaches are susceptible to interference from high-level noise signals, and the network architectures often prioritize pixel-level image reconstruction while lacking attention to holistic geospatial feature representation. In response, we propose a dual-branch single-image self-supervised denoising network based on Laplacian nonlocal self-similarity sampling for high-level noise images. In this network, we leverage the noise sensitivity of the Laplacian operator and the nonlocal self-similarity of images to design a nonlocal sampling strategy, which can generate numerous pseudo-samples only using a single high-level noisy image. Furthermore, we develop a dual-branch edge-enhanced convolutional neural network (CNN) as the denoising model, consisting of a pixel-level local feature (PLF) branch and an edge-guided global feature branch. The PLF branch achieves pixel-wise image reconstruction by extracting local features from neighboring pixels, while the edge-guided global feature branch employs a Sobel operator-based edge enhancement to guide the network in extracting global geospatial features from noisy images. This network constrains edge-smoothing artifacts during image reconstruction and ensures consistency of overall image features throughout the process. Since this method does not require precise prior assumptions about noise distribution or clean training images, it can be applied to various noise types and different noise levels. Extensive experimental results on multiple noise types and different noise intensities, along with tests on downstream classification tasks, demonstrate that the proposed method achieves particularly remarkable superiority under high-level Gaussian noise and mixed noise scenarios, while maintaining competitive performance in low-noise, pure Poisson noise, and real-noise conditions.
author: Minghao Liu, Huaxiang Zhang, Yikun Liu, Zhihao Li, Gongping Yang

title: Land Surface Temperature Downscaling via Deep Cross-Fusion of Spatiotemporal Features
content: Land surface temperature (LST) serves as a critical parameter for understanding surface energy exchange processes in urban areas. Acquiring LST data with a high spatial resolution is vital for analyzing urban thermal environments. However, most existing downscaling methods rely on pixel-level statistical or machine-learning models, and even recent deep-learning-based methods still have difficulty in preserving thermal consistency and recovering fine spatial details over highly heterogeneous urban surfaces. To overcome this challenge, this article proposes a deep spatiotemporal cross-fusion (SCF) framework for robust LST downscaling from 1 km to 100 m. First, a diverse set of spatiotemporal feature factors is extracted from multisource data. Then, an SCF network (SCF-Net) with multiple attention modules is designed to model the nonlinear relationships between LST and heterogeneous spatiotemporal features. Guided by a global-local hybrid loss, SCF-Net can accurately reconstruct fine-scale LST in complex urban areas. Extensive experiments across representative urban regions demonstrate that SCF-Net outperforms several state-of-the-art traditional and deep-learning-based methods. Quantitatively, SCF-Net achieves superior performance with R ${}^{2} =0.85$ , correlation coefficient (CC) = 0.92, and root-mean-square error (RMSE) = 0.82 K. Compared with the second-best method, it further reduces the RMSE by 0.20 K and improves R2 and the CC by 0.09 and 0.05, respectively. Moreover, SCF-Net exhibits exceptional robustness in recovering spatial textures and maintaining stable thermal patterns across different seasons and heterogeneous land covers, highlighting its effectiveness for high-resolution urban thermal-environment monitoring.
author: Huifang Li, Chenglin Shao, Lei Zhou, Huanfeng Shen

title: A Novel DOA Estimation Algorithm for Directional Borehole Radar Based on the Subspace Method and Full-Wave Simulation
content: Directional borehole radar (D-BHR) can acquire the 3-D information of subsurface targets from a single borehole. However, there are several challenges in D-BHR when applying direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation to obtain the directional information of the subsurface targets. This article addresses the challenges in DOA estimation for uniform circular antenna array (UCAA)-based D-BHR, including wideband signals, electromagnetic coupling interference, and inhomogeneous borehole interference. We propose a modified DOA estimation algorithm integrating the subspace method with full-wave simulation. The proposed method begins by constructing a simulation model based on prior information. Next, the angular responses of the antenna array are acquired through full-wave simulation. Subsequently, the steering vector is derived by applying subspace decomposition to the array responses to extract the signal subspace. The steering vector obtained by combining full-wave simulation and subspace methods is referred to as the subspace-based universal steering vector (S-USV). Finally, DOA estimation is performed based on the orthogonality between the steering vector and the noise subspace of the signal. To address the high computational cost of the full-wave simulation part, we propose a highly efficient algorithm based on the Lorentz reciprocity theorem, which is applicable to various full-wave simulation methods. We simulated a fracture model and generated 3-D imaging results using the proposed method. Compared with conventional methods, the proposed approach demonstrates several advantages, including stronger robustness against interference, higher computational efficiency, and lower memory requirements.
author: Sen Tian, Jianfu Ni, Sixin Liu, Qiancheng Zhao, Xianghao Liu, Mingqi Hu

title: Impact of Atmosphere on Geostationary SAR: Performance Analysis and Optimization
content: Geosynchronous synthetic aperture radar (GEO-SAR) offers a unique capability for wide-area monitoring with persistent observation, leveraging long integration times to compensate for severe path losses at large sensor–target distances. However, spatial and temporal variations in both the troposphere and ionosphere during these long integrations hinder effective azimuth focusing. This article addresses a geosynchronous synthetic aperture radar (SAR) system whose orbit complies with International Telecommunication Union (ITU) regulations for geostationary platforms. Specifically, it investigates optimizing the satellite orbit under constraints imposed by available orbital slots and regulatory requirements. The upper limit on the velocity is determined by additive noise sources (thermal noise, radio frequency interference, and so on), while the lower limit is constrained by the phase noise due to the atmospheric turbulence. The article first develops a statistical model describing the joint variability of ionospheric and tropospheric delays. It then provides a statistical characterization of atmospheric turbulence, which is quantitatively evaluated through the analysis of large datasets obtained from meteorological measurements and global navigation satellite system (GNSS)-based observations. The combination of the performance model and the statistical characterization of atmospheric turbulence enables the optimization of both the system and the satellite orbit. Results show that while a ±0.1° ITU station-keeping box allows for mission feasibility, expanding the tolerance to ±0.5°—still within experimental ITU limits—yields significantly better performance.
author: Matteo Monti, Alessandro Gatti, Martina Lagasio, Andrea Monti Guarnieri

title: A Phase Correlation-Based Time-Series InSAR Algorithm
content: Coherence loss due to changes in surface scattering properties presents a significant challenge for deformation mapping using interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR). A key objective in time-series InSAR processing is identifying ground targets that remain relatively stable over time. Many existing time-series InSAR algorithms rely on the statistical analysis of individual scatterers. In this study, we introduce a novel phase correlation-based InSAR algorithm that exploits the spatial continuity of time-series phase measurements. We develop a statistical model for the wrapped phase spatial difference and identify spatially correlated pixels (SCPs)—pixels that exhibit strong spatial correlation with neighboring pixels, quantified using the temporal coherence of their relative wrapped phase histories. Bayesian inference is employed to select these SCPs, which are then processed using an adaptive filter weighted by the estimated temporal coherence with neighboring pixels. We apply the proposed algorithm to C-band Sentinel-1 data over the Greater Houston Area (GHA) in Texas, United States. The results demonstrate the algorithm’s ability to effectively identify coherent scatterers and retrieve deformation signals even in challenging conditions, offering improved spatial coverage and phase quality.
author: Jiahui Wang, Jinwoo Kim, Zhong Lu

title: Are Deep Learning Models Trained on Synthetic Data Actually Reliable in InSAR? A Self-Supervised Learning Approach for InSAR Phase Denoising
content: Deep learning is revolutionizing the way synthetic aperture radar interferometry (InSAR) data are processed. Supervised deep learning models trained on synthetic datasets have demonstrated strong performance according to conventional evaluation metrics. However, in practice, many supervised InSAR phase denoising filters tend to oversmooth fringe patterns and generate ghost fringes in decorrelated regions, raising concerns about the reliability of models trained solely on synthetic data in real-world applications. In this work, we introduce Noise2Fringe, an unsupervised learning framework that enables the direct training of denoising filters on noisy interferograms, eliminating the need for clean references, while achieving performance comparable to that of supervised models. This framework allows a fair comparison between models trained exclusively on real versus synthetic data, independent of the training scheme. On synthetic benchmarks, models trained on real data appear to underperform compared to those trained on synthetic data. However, in real-world interferograms, the opposite holds: synthetic-trained models oversmooth and generate ghost fringes, whereas real-trained models avoid these artifacts. These findings reveal: 1) a much larger gap between synthetic and real data than previously assumed; 2) an overestimation of the effectiveness of synthetic-trained models; and 3) deficiencies in current evaluation methods that rely heavily on simulated benchmarks. We further find that incorporating auxiliary information, such as the amplitude dispersion index (ADI), into the input can enhance denoising performance. Overall, this study demonstrates that models trained solely on synthetic data may be far less reliable in real-world applications than suggested by their benchmark results and emphasizes that generating more realistic training datasets should take precedence over designing increasingly complex model architectures.
author: Kang Liang, Zhong Lu, Jinwoo Kim

title: A High-Band Phase Synchronization Approach for Distributed UAV SAR
content: To achieve the multistatic phase synchronization of distributed uncrewed aerial vehicle synthetic aperture radar (UAV SAR), the two-way direct wave links are established between every two radar units and the time-varying phase fluctuations in bistatic echoes can be eliminated utilizing the traditional two-way phase synchronization method. However, the vibration environment of the UAV platform causes the degradation of the frequency source stability. Especially at high-carrier frequencies, the phase fluctuations doubling to RF terminal break the phase unwrapping condition, and the time-varying  $\pi $ -ambiguity is introduced in phase synchronization error estimation value that causes the SAR image defocused. In addition, to satisfy the general baseline design requirements, there may be direct wave link obstruction between stations, resulting in interruption of direct wave reception and subsequent failure of phase synchronization. Even if the direct wave link remains unobstructed, potential multipath effects can degrade synchronization accuracy. To address the above issues, a reliable distributed UAV SAR high-band phase synchronization approach is proposed, in which a coarse-to-fine two-stage phase synchronization is performed based on two-way direct waves and echoes, respectively. In the coarse synchronization stage, the time-varying  $\pi $ -ambiguity after unwrapping is effectively suppressed using two-way multiplication and local unwrapping techniques, and full-link phase synchronization is achieved utilizing the phase triangle under conditions of partially obstructed direct-wave links. In the fine synchronization stage, the two-way echo of the calibration target is utilized instead of the full scene echo to avoid multipath effects in the direct-wave link, thereby achieving higher phase synchronization accuracy. This approach has been applied in the high-band distributed UAV SAR system, and the real measured data shows the effectiveness of the approach.
author: Junjie Yan, Linghao Li, Pucheng Li, Zegang Ding, Yibo Gao, Yibo Liu

title: Static Calibration of the KaRIn Radar for the SWOT Mission
content: This article discusses the definition and estimation of static calibration parameters for the Ka-band Radar Interferometer (KaRIn) instrument of the Surface Water and Ocean Topography (SWOT) mission. In the crucial pitch axis, the antenna pointing biases were calibrated to within a small fraction of the azimuthal beamwidth using the observed Doppler centroid. Delays in the radar electronics were calibrated using corner reflectors that were deployed in North America. The interferometric phase was calibrated by comparing large amounts of tide-corrected ocean data against the mean sea surface (MSS). After applying static calibration, there is less than 1 cm of residual systematic height error in the KaRIn interferometric height measurement, in addition to the slow time-varying error that is corrected by the crossover correction. The high-rate (HR) and low-rate (LR) height measurements agree to within 1 cm. Absolute radiometric calibration was done by comparison against global precipitation measurement (GPM) data, and the resulting  $\sigma ^{0}$  measurements are self-consistent to better than 1 dB. We find that a single set of static calibration parameters can be used for both the calibration and science orbits, and the parameters are stable on time scales of over a year.
author: Albert Chen, Curtis Chen, Xiaoqing Wu, Jan-Willem De Bleser, Alexander Fore, Brent Williams, Alexander Corben, Bryan Stiles, Shailen Desai

title: SDB-Mamba: Cross-Scale Collaborative Visual State Space Network for Bathymetric Retrieval From Subsurface Reflectance of Satellite Multispectral Imagery
content: Although subsurface-reflectance-based satellite-derived bathymetry (SDB) effectively mitigates sun-glitter effects, current methods struggle to disentangle subtle bathymetric features from complex background environmental noise, severely limiting their generalization across diverse waters. This limitation fundamentally stems from the inability of existing architectures to simultaneously capture high-frequency local textures and long-range semantic dependencies required for precise depth inference in degraded optical environments. To address this challenge, SDB-Mamba, a cross-scale collaborative visual state space (VSS) network, is presented for robust bathymetric retrieval from multispectral imagery. First, a dual-encoder Mamba-ResNet architecture is developed to synergize the linear complexity of VSS models for modeling global contextual dependencies with residual connections for preserving fine-grained local details. Second, to enhance feature discriminability, a spatial attention block (SAB) is integrated to suppress environmental noise in spatially heterogeneous regions, while a channel attention block (CAB) is employed to adaptively recalibrates channel weights based on local optical water characteristics. SDB-Mamba was rigorously evaluated across six diverse study areas with distinct optical properties and bottom types, including unseen domains for external testing. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves mean absolute errors (MAEs) of 0.36–0.64 m, reducing root mean square error (RMSE) by up to 86% compared to SwinUNet and improving peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) by 66% relative to TransNet. Ablation studies confirm the critical role of the VSS, SAB, and CAB components, which improved structural similarity index measure (SSIM) by 25%, 68%, and 62%, respectively, establishing SDB-Mamba as a significant advancement in robust, high-precision nearshore bathymetry.
author: Guoqing Zhou, Jiangying Wu, Yao Pu, Jón Atli Benediktsson

title: UAV-Based Multispectral Object Tracking With Positive–Negative Prompt Mining Network
content: Multispectral imaging (MSI) based on uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) can capture valuable spectral, thereby improving the capability of object tracking. However, most existing multispectral (MS) trackers focus on feature matching of the foreground target, leading to target loss due to sparse features and strong interference under complex backgrounds. To address this challenge, we propose the positive–negative prompt mining network (PNPM-Net), which models both background interference and target features as structured negative and positive prompts to enhance feature discriminability under complex scenarios. It mainly consists of three key components: spatial–spectral–temporal attention mining (SSTAM), positive–negative joint mining (PNJM) module, and dual-incentive prompt modulation (DIPM) module. First, we propose SSTAM for adaptive spectral channel reweighting and spatial reshaping, thereby augmenting the spatial–spectral separability between target and background signatures under complex dynamic scenarios. Second, we introduce PNJM to generate contrastive priors modeling the positive and negative prompts. Finally, we develop DIPM to employ these priors for feature enhancement and background suppression, thereby increasing the interclass discriminativeness between target and background. Experiments show PNPM-Net achieves 71.8% AUC and 88.1% DP@20, surpassing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by 18.5% and 9.2%, respectively, which demonstrate the strong potential in UAV-based MS object tracking (MOT) area.
author: Xiang Teng, Dong Zhao, Pei Xiang, Xin Yu, Zhuanfeng Li, Chih-Chung Hsu, Tianfang Yang, Huixin Zhou, Jinchang Ren

title: Fine-Grained Text and Spectral Attribute Supervised Semantic Network for Hyperspectral Image Zero-Shot Domain Generalization
content: Cross-scene hyperspectral image (HSI) classification has attracted increasing attention due to domain shifts caused by distribution differences and label scarcity. However, most domain generalization (DG) methods assume that classes are shared across domains, which fails when novel categories appear. Zero-shot DG (ZSDG) is thus essential to address both semantic and distribution shifts. Recent advances in vision-language foundation models (VLFMs) offer promising solutions to this challenge. In this article, we propose a ZSDG method, FTSA, which efficiently fine-tunes the CLIP model via prompt learning (PL) to extract a domain-invariant semantic space from textual and spectral modalities. Specifically, we introduce two interclass shared learnable prompts in the text branch. To guide PL of shared contextual features, we use large language model (LLM)-derived land-cover priors and intrinsic spectral attributes as dual complementary supervision signals. By designing several chain-of-thought (CoT) templates, the LLM automatically generates well-decoupled, fine-grained land-cover descriptions. To compensate for the weak representation of highly nonlinear spectral correlations in text, we design the spectral attributes local cache (SALC) module, construct a fixed attribute cache, and introduce an attribute update strategy based on local sparse matching to dynamically aggregate generic spectral visual attributes. Parallel prompts and category names are concatenated to align the corresponding textual and spectral features during training, implicitly learning universal spatial–spectral semantics. On multiple datasets, FTSA achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating robustness and zero-shot generalization. The code will be released as open source at https://github.com/blackdyc/FTSA
author: Yuchen Deng, Yang Xu, Le Sun, Zhihui Wei, Qian Du, Zebin Wu

title: Multimodal Prompt Tuning With Modified Knowledge Distillation for the Joint Classification of Hyperspectral Image and LiDAR Data
content: While pretrained foundation models (FMs) exhibit remarkable generalization across diverse tasks, their full fine-tuning under limited labeled data remains prohibitively expensive and prone to performance degradation or overfitting. To mitigate these challenges in the context of multimodal remote sensing image classification, we propose multimodal prompt tuning with modified knowledge distillation (MPT-MKD), a parameter-efficient learning framework enabling accurate class predictions while updating only a small fraction of model parameters. Prompt tuning is adapted to multimodal remote sensing by injecting modality-specific learnable prompts (spectral and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) prompts) into a student FM, whereby cross-modal interactions are captured through self-attention. In addition, a knowledge distillation (KD) scheme is designed that leverages both limited labeled samples and abundant unlabeled data, where a teacher FM, with parameters frozen except for a learnable classifier, generates soft pseudo-labels for the student FM. To mitigate potential inaccuracies in the logits predicted by the teacher FM, a similarity- and class-consistency-based selection strategy is employed to identify a more reliable subset of unlabeled samples and to reconstruct modified soft targets, thereby alleviating the adverse effects of noisy distillation signals. Experimental results on three real hyperspectral image (HSI) and LiDAR datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
author: Yi Kong, Shaocai Yu, Yuhu Cheng, C. L. Philip Chen, Xuesong Wang

title: CLCNet: A Curriculum-Guided Contrastive Learning Network With Spectral-Hierarchical Attention for Hyperspectral-LiDAR Image Classification
content: The scarcity of labeled samples has long constrained hyperspectral–light detection and ranging (LiDAR) fusion for land-cover classification, making contrastive learning a promising paradigm. However, existing methods still suffer from false-negative interference and insufficient modeling of hard negatives, which degrade representation quality. To address these challenges, we propose a curriculum-learning-based contrastive learning network (CLCNet), which enhances contrastive learning from two perspectives. First, a spectral–hierarchical multihead attention (MHA) mechanism reorders and groups spectral channels to decouple attention across groups and improve feature discriminability. Second, a curriculum-aware dynamic negative sampling (curriculum-DNS) strategy adaptively filters potential false negatives and progressively emphasizes hard negatives, stabilizing convergence and enhancing representation learning. In addition, we propose a spectral–hierarchical dual-stage fusion encoder that incorporates LiDAR edge priors at shallow layers and elevation cues at deeper stages, enabling spatially aware and complementary multimodal fusion. Comprehensive experiments across three public benchmark datasets highlight the robustness and performance advantages of our network. The reference code for our work can be accessed at https://github.com/chaoreee/CLCNet
author: Changzhe Jiao, Chao Hu, Xu Tang, Hao Zhu, Licheng Jiao

title: Diffusion Feature Completion-Driven Textual Dual Distillation for Multimodal Remote Sensing Classification With Missing Modality
content: In recent years, research on joint classification of multimodal remote sensing data has achieved remarkable progress. However, constrained by imaging conditions or sensor issues, missing modality frequently occurs in practice. Knowledge distillation, as one of the mainstream strategies to deal with missing modality, is widely used due to its excellent transfer ability. However, most existing knowledge distillation methods focus on directly transferring available modal information through feature alignment or predictive constraints, without explicitly modeling or refining the latent semantic structure of missing modality. Furthermore, the heterogeneity between different modalities makes effective alignment difficult through direct transfer, thus limiting the utilization of cross-modal complementary information and consequently hindering model performance improvement in remote sensing scenarios with missing modality. Therefore, in this article, we propose a diffusion feature completion-driven textual dual distillation model (DFC-TD2) for multimodal remote sensing image classification with missing modality. This model explicitly models the missing modality, introduces textual semantic information to guide cross-modal feature transfer, and achieves multiconstraint collaborative optimization under the supervision of classification labels, thereby effectively improving the overall performance of multimodal classification tasks with missing modality. Specifically, a shared-constrained diffusion feature completion network (SCFC) is designed, which uses shared features extracted and frozen from multimodal data as constraints to guide the diffusion model to effectively model and complete missing modal features, thereby generating semantically consistent and more discriminative modal representations. Furthermore, a text-guided dual distillation network (TGD2) is designed to guide cross-modal feature alignment using text semantics and combine it with classification label supervision to construct a feature-label dual constraint, which effectively improves feature discriminativeness and supervision efficiency, thereby mitigating cross-modal representation heterogeneity and improving the stability and effectiveness of knowledge transfer. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of this method in dealing with missing modality. The code is available at: https://github.com/Jiahuiqu/DFC-TD2.
author: Dan Xu, Wenqian Dong, Song Xiao, Jiahui Qu, Yunsong Li

title: Spatial–Spectral Enhancement Contrastive Learning Network for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Few-shot hyperspectral image (HSI) classification plays a crucial role in the field of remote sensing with the rapid development of deep learning. However, few-shot HSI classification remains challenging. When labeled samples are scarce, severe overfitting arises from limited feature generalization ability in effectively capturing and fusing discriminative spatial–spectral features. In this study, a spatial–spectral enhancement contrastive learning network (SSECLN) is proposed for few-shot HSI classification. This network includes three modules: spatial–spectral contrastive learning (SSCL), spatial–spectral cross-attention (SSCA), and partial multikernel convolution (PMC). The SSCL includes multiscale spatial enhancement (MSE), bidirectional spectral enhancement (SSE), and batch contrastive learning (BCL). The SSE extracts spatial structural information at different scales and combines forward and backward spectral context to fully explore spectral dependencies. By introducing same-class sample constraints, BCL not only improves intraclass compactness and interclass separability but also alleviates overfitting problems in few-shot scenarios. The SSCA realizes the dynamic interaction of local spatial information with global spectral information, adaptively accommodating the coupled spatial–spectral features through its inherent mechanism. The PMC is used to extract important spatial–spectral features to improve model efficiency. The results show that the overall accuracy is increased by 5.86%–34.59% (IP), 0.96%–24.28% (KSC), 2.39%–23.40% (Salinas), and 3.86%–35.85% (UP) by using the comparison of SSECLN and the state-of-the-art approaches. Codes are available at https://github.com/Zhangshizhe04/SSECLN
author: Shizhe Zhang, Lina Yang, Yaojun Cai, Yifeng Tan, Wanxing Zhu, Thomas Xinzhang Wu, Xichun Li, Yan Yuan Tang

title: DeepGrounder: Generalized Reasoning for MLLM-Based Remote Sensing Visual Grounding
content: Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to accurately localize target regions in remote sensing imagery based on natural language descriptions. Existing approaches leveraging multimodal large language models (MLLMs) predominantly rely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT). While SFT enhances instruction-following capabilities, it often fails to foster genuine reasoning due to overlearning statistical correlations among image features, text, and coordinates. This limitation severely hampers generalization in both in-domain scenarios with imbalanced category difficulty and cross-domain scenarios with distribution shifts. To address this, we propose DeepGrounder, a novel RSVG method designed for robust generalization and reasoning. DeepGrounder employs a two-stage training strategy: the first stage uses SFT to inject domain knowledge and establish foundational spatial perception; the second stage introduces reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) via group relative policy optimization (GRPO) to stimulate structured reasoning. To provide stable and verifiable learning signals for multimodal reasoning, we formulate a hybrid verifiable reward mechanism encompassing reasoning format standardization, geometric localization accuracy, and vision-language semantic consistency. This mechanism guides the model from passive mapping to active reasoning. Experiments on RRSIS-D and DIOR-RSVG datasets show that DeepGrounder significantly outperforms SFT-based baselines, particularly on challenging categories. Notably, its zero-shot performance on VRSBench surpasses models specifically fine-tuned on that dataset, directly validating its superior cross-domain generalization capabilities. The code will be available at https://github.com/ULTA-Web/DeepGrounder
author: Jiafan Zhang, Keyan Chen, Chenyang Liu, Baihong Lin, Zhengxia Zou, Zhenwei Shi

title: Transferability Reinforcement of Adversarial Attacks for Remote Sensing Image Classification via Hierarchical Transformation Composition
content: Adversarial attacks constitute an effective means of evaluating model robustness and revealing intrinsic weaknesses. Since practical model deployment typically adheres to black-box settings, existing attack methods often employ multiple input transformations to enhance the transferability of adversarial examples. However, remote sensing images often exhibit ambiguous foreground–background distinctions and various geospatial discrepancies, resulting in high model specificity in discriminative cues for classification. Consequently, the input patterns simulated through fixed transformation schemes are insufficient to prevent overfitting to the source model, thereby limiting the transferability of the generated adversarial examples. To solve this issue, we propose a hierarchical transformation composition (HTC) framework that reinforces adversarial transferability through a coordinated action execution (CAE) strategy. Specifically, the determination of input transformations is formulated as an adaptive action selection procedure, which is progressively executed by multilevel policy networks. Then, the policy networks are iteratively updated via proximal policy optimization (PPO) based on the advantage estimates from a shared value network. Moreover, a hybrid reward mechanism (HRM) is introduced to dynamically integrate loss information from both feature and output layers. Rather than directly imposing directional constraints on gradient calculation, the supervision is shifted to the optimization of policy networks, which prevents the sacrifice of intrinsic attack capacity while enhancing transferability. Extensive experiments on the UCM and SIRI-WHU datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance across various model architectures. The code will be released at https://github.com/fuyimin96/HTC upon acceptance.
author: Yimin Fu, Yuefeng Bai, Jialin Lyu, Baicheng Pan, Zhunga Liu, Michael K. Ng

title: SAINet: Scale-Aware Adaptive Interactive Semantic Segmentation Network for Remote Sensing Scenarios
content: In the semantic segmentation of remote sensing images (RSIs), heterogeneous modalities often exhibit distribution discrepancies and noise interference. How to effectively mitigate modality gaps and reduce noise while fully exploiting the complementary information across multimodal data remains a challenging problem. To tackle this issue, a scale-aware adaptive interactive network (SAINet) was proposed for the semantic segmentation of remote sensing scenes. First, SAINet employs an adaptive feature interaction (AFI) module to align features from optical RSIs and digital surface models (DSMs) at both spatial and channel levels, thereby alleviating cross-modal inconsistency and enhancing the complementary utilization of multimodal information. Second, by introducing hierarchical contextual semantic representations, SAINet constructs a multiscale context aggregation (MSCA) module, which strengthens the model’s capability to perceive objects at different scales as well as complex land-cover distributions. Furthermore, a symmetric dilated spatial mixer (SDSM) is designed to effectively balance local and global dependency modeling, while maintaining favorable computational efficiency and stability when processing high-resolution inputs. Finally, comprehensive experiments are performed on two widely used segmentation datasets, namely, Vaihingen and Potsdam, and the obtained results indicate that SAINet delivers superior segmentation performance.
author: Shuaiqi Liu, Zhonghao Deng, Jiahui Cao, Bing Li, Yudong Zhang, Hui Wang

title: CTTF: Cross-Temporal Tri-Difference Fusion With Mamba for Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: Mamba has recently been widely applied to remote sensing change detection (CD) tasks due to its ability to efficiently represent long-range dependencies with nearly linear computational complexity. However, existing Mamba-based CD methods focus primarily on global modeling during the decoding stage, where insufficient attention to fine-grained details causes blurred change boundaries. In addition, most existing methods rely solely on a single type of differential feature (e.g., signed difference or absolute difference), ignoring the complementary information among different types of differential features, which makes them prone to missed or false detections under registration errors.
