To address these issues, this article proposes a decoder-side improved framework named cross-temporal tri-difference fusion (CTTF). It consists of a global–local–point (GLP) Block and a signed–absolute–sum fusion (SASF) Module, enabling accurate boundary feature detection, thereby achieving more consistent and reliable change discrimination. Specifically, the GLP Block decouples and models boundary details, global semantics, and information anchors, achieving a dynamic balance between local enhancement and semantic fidelity, thereby significantly improving the boundary feature perception. The SASF constructs a tri-differential mechanism, where the sum difference serves as a stability prior to perform multiscale calibration between the signed difference and the absolute difference. This design compensates for the loss of fine structural and consistency information caused by relying on a single differential domain, effectively reducing both missed and false detections. The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the WHU-CD, LEVIR-CD, SYSU-CD, and CLCD datasets. Ablation experiments further validate the effectiveness of the proposed SASF Module and GLP Block.
author: Shuying Li, Haolan Lei, Yuemei Qin, Chuang Yang

title: Spatial-Frequency Enhancement and Scale-Decoupled Refinement Network for Salient Object Detection in ORSI
content: Optical remote sensing image salient object detection (ORSI-SOD) focuses on identifying and localizing visually distinctive regions within complex scenes characterized by intricate structures and cluttered backgrounds. This task presents significant challenges due to substantial scale variations among objects, inherent ambiguity in target appearance, pervasive background noise interference, and edge degradation caused by shadow effects. To address these challenges, we propose a novel spatial-frequency enhancement and scale-decoupled refinement network (SESRNet). The SESRNet synergistically combines three key components: dynamic state modeling, frequency-aware feature representation, and structure-enhanced attention mechanisms. Our approach specifically overcomes the limitations of conventional frequency-domain modeling while effectively capturing long-range contextual dependencies, thereby significantly improving salient object detection (SOD) accuracy in optical remote sensing imagery. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that SESRNet outperforms the current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods across three public datasets. Code and results will be available at https://github.com/hjy0518/SESRNet/
author: Jinyu Han, Mengyin Wang, Chenglong Zheng, Yaoyao Hou, Fuming Sun

title: A Composite Doppler Spectrum of Forward Scattered GNSS Signal From Sea Surface and Its Application in Retrieving Coastal Wind Vector
content: Conventional methodologies for coastal Global Navigation Satellite System-Reflectometry (GNSS-R) are derived from the assumption of pure specular reflection, which is typically modeled using the Kirchhoff Approximation (KA). Such methods are generally considered insensitive to wind direction, thereby posing a challenge for wind direction retrieval in coastal GNSS-R. This article introduces a new theoretical assumption, wherein the signal received by a sea-looking antenna is regarded as a synthesis of specular reflection and off-specular scattering. The off-specular component follows the Bragg scattering mechanism and is modeled using the small slope approximation (SSA). Based on this assumption, a composite spectrum is theoretically derived, which effectively captures the multimodal characteristics observed in actual measured spectra. Within this composite spectrum, three key spectral characteristics, namely, the spectral width of specular reflection, the peak ratio of the positive and negative Bragg spectral components, and wave-motion-induced Doppler shift, are uniquely dependent on both wind speed and wind direction. An iterative Gaussian fitting method is proposed to separate the spectra corresponding to specular reflection and Bragg scattering, from which these three spectral characteristics are subsequently estimated. Experimental validation supports the theoretical assumption and derivation. Two Bragg scattering components, corresponding to diffraction orders 1 and 2, are successfully extracted. The estimated spectral characteristics exhibit consistent dependencies on wind speed and direction with the theoretical predictions, confirming the coexistence of specular reflection and Bragg scattering in forward GNSS-R observations. A least-square optimization approach is employed to simultaneously retrieve wind speed and direction. Comparisons between the retrieved and in situ wind vectors demonstrate good agreement. In summary, this article proposes a promising forward-scattered signal spectrum model that effectively addresses the challenge in simultaneously retrieving wind speed and direction in coastal GNSS-R.
author: Feng Wang, Chuanrui Tan, Xiangchao Ma, Weichen Sun, Linfeng Wang, Faping Lu, Zhichao Xu, Jie Li, Lei Yang, Bo Zhang, Dongkai Yang

title: Imaging-Guided Pattern Optimization for Seismic Acquisition Geometries Design With Deep Learning
content: The primary purpose of seismic acquisition geometry design is to illuminate the subsurface to identify potential energy reservoirs. Traditional acquisition design methods include uniform, random, and jittered patterns. Uniform patterns can be impractical due to physical constraints, and irregular patterns can introduce incoherent noise to the data through aliasing. Deep learning (DL) methods have focused on data-driven approaches, regularized by seismic signal quality reconstruction to design undersampled patterns. However, they frequently overlook imaging performance under geological variability. In this work, we introduce imaging-guided seismic pattern optimization (ISPO), a novel DL-based framework. ISPO requires a reference velocity model, from which a forward operator generates modeled shot gathers. A denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) then produces structurally diverse yet geologically consistent perturbations to the velocity model. These perturbations are used to train an imaging or inversion operator that maps shot gathers to velocity models. The central objective is to optimize receiver and/or source positions using a binary layer, guided by the estimated quality of the velocity models obtained from the optimized pattern, while allowing for undersampling. ISPO was evaluated across three acquisition scenarios—receiver-only, source-only, and simultaneous undersampling. ISPO consistently outperforms both classical and DL-based baselines, including uniform, jittered, random, and end-to-end reconstruction-driven designs. The method yields superior performance in structural similarity index (SSIM), mean-squared error (mse), and perceptual metrics, while preserving key structural features of the velocity model even at low acquired rates.
author: Ana Mantilla-Dulcey, Henry Arguello

title: Near Real-Time Single-Image SAR Flood Mapping With Synthetic Pre-Event Reference: On-Demand COSMO-SkyMed Case Study
content: Near-real-time (NRT) flood mapping from on-demand synthetic aperture radar (SAR) data is often hindered by the lack of a suitable pre-event image in the archive. Under emergency conditions, searching for a recent, radiometrically comparable pre-event acquisition can be unsuccessful, limiting change-detection approaches and reducing flood mapping accuracy. This article presents a new NRT framework to generate a physically consistent synthetic pre-event baseline, enabling effective bitemporal flood mapping with a single postevent SAR image. First, Copernicus global land service surface soil moisture (SSM, 1 km) is downscaled to sentinel-1 geometry (S1, ∼20 m) using the same S1 data from which SSM is derived. The downscaled SSM is then converted to volumetric soil moisture using a porosity map derived from bulk density data. Second, a pre-event SAR image is simulated via a semi-empirical backscattering model. The simulation accounts for surface roughness uncertainty, which is propagated through three scenarios (smooth, medium, rough). Finally, the synthetic baseline is regridded to the postevent SAR acquisition and integrated into the AUTOWADE2.0 processor, a fully automated flood mapping algorithm described in prior work, to generate flood extent maps through change detection. Experiments on recent Italian flood events demonstrate that the proposed workflow produces pre-event backscatter data that enable fast and reliable SAR-based flood mapping when real pre-event data are unavailable.
author: Luca Pulvirenti, Giuseppe Squicciarino, Silvia Puca

title: MISR: Robust Multimodal Remote Image Matching of Earth and Planetary Using Modality-Invariant Structural Representation
content: Accurate image matching is a crucial prerequisite for multimodal data fusion in Earth observation and serves as a foundational technology for deep-space exploration. While Earth multimodal remote sensing images (EMRSIs) often suffer from severe nonlinear radiometric differences and geometric deformations, planetary multimodal remote sensing images (PMRSIs) introduce additional challenges due to disparate sensing modalities and predominantly weak-texture surfaces. To address these cross-domain challenges, we propose a modality-invariant structural representation (MISR) framework designed for robust matching of both EMRSIs and PMRSIs. The MISR framework consists of two core modules. First, a novel image scale space is constructed using weighted side-window based gradient guided filtering, which facilitates effective noise suppression while preserving essential structural and edge features. Second, a multiorder structural representation is introduced via the multiorder gradient location and orientation histogram (MGLOH) descriptor, which integrates a weighted dominant orientation assignment with an enhanced spatial configuration. By fusing first- and second-order gradient responses, MGLOH offers improved rotation invariance and stronger feature discrimination. Extensive experiments conducted on both Earth and planetary datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior matching accuracy and robustness compared to state-of-the-art approaches.
author: Bai Zhu, Wanning Chen, Jianing Cao, Mengmeng Wang, Xu Liu, Yu Guo, Bo-Hui Tang

title: Toward Collaborative Multimodal Information Perception: A Hierarchical Feature Alignment Network and Acoustic-Optical Dataset for Underwater Object Detection
content: In the realm of Earth observation, underwater target detection serves as a critical component for characterizing aquatic environments. However, existing acoustic-optical collaborative methods face persistent challenges, including the high calibration difficulty across different modalities and unsatisfactory feature alignment. A core difficulty lies in enabling the network to adaptively capture intrinsic dependencies and achieve efficient fusion of multimodal information during the learning process. To address this, this article proposes an object detection method with adaptive cross-modal feature alignment. Specifically, the method establishes automatic alignment relationships between sonar and optical images through fine-grained and instance-level feature fusion. Furthermore, a novel fusion strategy is designed to enable efficient acoustic-optical information fusion via same-layer feature learning. Finally, to mitigate the issues of scarcity and low quality of multimodal underwater datasets, a data acquisition platform is constructed, and a strictly spatiotemporally synchronized sonar-optical dataset is built. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method simultaneously enhances detection performance on both sonar and optical images. On the created dataset, the proposed method achieves mAP@0.5 scores of 86.9% and 94.4% for acoustic-optical collaborative detection, thereby outperforming mainstream approaches. In addition, the robustness and generalization of the proposed method are verified in other multimodal object detection scenarios.
author: Jing Chen, Junjie Xie, Mingxin Liu, Hao Wang, Cong Lin

title: Adaptive Context-Aware Localization Network for Remote Sensing Object Detection Under Haze and Class Imbalance
content: Object detection in optical remote sensing imagery is severely challenged by haze degradation and long-tailed category imbalance, which result in blurred appearances, noise interference, and frequent missed detections of minority classes. These challenges hinder robust feature representation, accurate cross-scale localization, and balanced optimization. To address these issues, we propose ACLNet, a context-aware feature-enhancement and adaptive-localization-optimization framework for robust remote sensing object detection. Specifically, we first propose a context-aware poly-kernel module, which enhances mid- to high-level feature representations via parallel multikernel modeling and axial context modulation to compensate for haze-induced detail loss. Subsequently, we introduce a dual-stream dynamic feature fusion mechanism, which dynamically fuses local structural cues with global dependencies, to improve cross-scale alignment and discriminative consistency in complex scenes. Furthermore, we adopt an adaptive differential modulation localization loss that suppresses noisy gradients while adaptively emphasizing medium-quality and hard samples, thereby enhancing localization stability for small objects and degraded scenes without increasing inference overhead. Experimental results show that ACLNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the DIOR, RSOD, and Hazy-Det datasets. In addition, ACLNet is highly deployable with only 10.7 M parameters.
author: Yingjun Wang, Xiaopeng Yang, Jiali Wang, Zheng Liang, Wenyi Zhao, Weidong Zhang

title: PINN-Inspired Self-Supervised Framework for Speckle Reduction in SAR Images
content: This article proposes a self-supervised synthetic aperture radar (SAR) despeckling framework for heterogeneous scenes and limited-data conditions. The proposed method combines a structure-aware dual-TV term and a speckle-aware self-supervised term, and links them through indicator-driven adaptive weighting derived from the physical and statistical characteristics of speckle noise and structural terrain information. From this viewpoint, the framework is a physics-informed neural network (PINN)-inspired, but not in the standard sense of solving an explicit physical forward partial differential equation. Instead, it adopts the PINN principle of embedding a governing residual-type constraint into training and extends it by modulating that constraint with pixel-wise indicators that reflect local structure–speckle properties. The proposed method was validated on both synthetic speckled SAR and real single-look satellite SAR data. In addition to ablation, noise-level robustness, and cross-validation analyses, representative mixed-terrain examples were examined from three complementary viewpoints: full-image restoration, speckle suppression in homogeneous regions, and structure preservation in densely textured regions. The results showed that the proposed framework provides a more favorable balance between speckle suppression and structural preservation than the compared baselines.
author: Seunghui Jang, Youngwook Kim

title: Evolving Detectors for Locating Methane Plumes in Hyperspectral Images
content: Identifying methane plumes in Earth observation image data is vital in environmental monitoring. This process is the key to locating methane emissions, which is critical for efforts aimed at reducing their contribution to climate change. However, the potential overlaps between methane and other gases in their spectral features make the identification of methane emitters challenging. Therefore, new, more effective and efficient algorithms are needed to address this issue. We propose incorporating methane enhancement maps extracted from image data using improved matched filtering into the feature vectors that undergo evolutionary selection before feeding them to supervised classification models. Therefore, to tackle the problem of methane detection and localization in hyperspectral images, we use a framework that combines evolutionary techniques for feature selection, followed by supervised machine learning for classifying hyperspectral superpixels as those containing or not containing methane. The experiments over the STARCOP benchmark dataset show that even if we exploit no more than three features, we obtain models whose quality, quantified as the area under the receiver operating curve, reaches 0.885 (with a sensitivity of 0.880).
author: Agata M. Wijata, Nicolas Longépé, Jakub Nalepa

title: HSANet: Hierarchical Semantic Alignment Network via Local–Global Adaptive Injection for Cropland Extraction
content: Delineation of farmland parcels is essential for precision agriculture and environmental monitoring, yet remains challenging due to spectral variability, boundary ambiguity, and complex background interference. We present HSANet, a high-precision semantic segmentation network built on three synergistic innovations. First, the local–global adaptive injection module fuses local and global features via dual paths: a local branch with the MogaMambaEnhancer captures fine-grained textures through gated bottleneck and bidirectional Mamba scanning, while a global branch with the GlobalMambaKernel models long-range dependences via visual state-space modeling and decomposed large-kernel convolution. Second, the global pyramid enhancer head replaces conventional refinement convolutions in the feature pyramid with a global convolution interaction mechanism, jointly enhancing multiscale representations with global context and local detail. Third, the anisotropic error diffusion and structural consolidation loss identifies structurally significant errors through iterative diffusion and applies modulated focal and geometric penalties to enforce pixel-level accuracy and topological coherence. Extensive experiments on the Fine-Grained Farmland dataset and Fields of the World dataset demonstrate that HSANet consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, achieving mean intersection over union/F1 of 87.25%/93.19% and 72.26%/83.90%, respectively, highlighting its potential for high-resolution agricultural mapping.
author: Bo Zhang, Guoyou Li, Qiang Zhang, Yaxiong Chen

title: Hyperspectral Weak Object Detection Using Homogeneous-Unique Feature Fusion
content: Hyperspectral object detection (HOD), as an emerging research field, has attracted growing attention due to its advantage for detecting objects in challenging scenes with weak objects. However, existing methods mainly depend on off-the-shelf RGB detectors for feature extraction, failing to fully exploit the abundant spectral context information in hyperspectral images (HSIs), which results in suboptimal detection performance, especially in the case of weak objects such as tiny or camouflaged objects. In this article, a method named HYPER-DET is proposed. First, to address the issue of insufficient utilization of spectral context information in feature extraction, a novel cross-modality feature extraction network is introduced to extract visual and spectral features simultaneously, thus enhancing the feature representation of weak objects. In the RGB branch of the proposed backbone, an end-to-end self-sensing regrouping module is presented to capture cross-band correlations and cluster optimal three-channel spectral band groups. In the HSI branch, a spatial–spectral feature extraction module is proposed to fully extract the complementary spatial and spectral information from HSIs. Second, to facilitate the efficient fusion of the cross-modal features between RGB and HSI modalities, a homogeneous-unique feature fusion module is designed, which adaptively extract and fuse both homogeneous and unique features across these two modalities. Finally, we introduce a new hyperspectral aerial tiny object dataset, HWOD-tiny. It is tailored for challenging scenarios that involve tiny uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) objects, providing a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating HOD methods in competitive settings. HWOD-tiny is the first dataset designed for hyperspectral aerial tiny object detection. Extensive experiments on multispectral UAV imagery dataset DroneVehicle, general HOD dataset HOD3K and the proposed datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed HYPER-DET, which outperforms existing state-of-the-art object detection methods.
author: Shaoyang Ma, Xun Huang, Hanzheng Wang, Bolun Cui, Zhicheng Shi, Wei Li

title: Data Distributional Attention-Based Heterogeneous Representation Learning for PolSAR Land Cover Classification
content: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) data are typically represented as a complex matrix, and different polarization decomposition parameters result in varying data distributions. Directly applying deep learning methods to extract features from PolSAR data, without accounting for the impact of these distributional differences, leads to incomplete representations. To address this issue, a data distributional attention-based heterogeneous representation learning method is proposed for PolSAR land cover classification. This method first analyzes the distribution patterns, followed by different polarization decomposition parameters. It combines the eigenvector polarization decomposition method to obtain parameter vectors that follow the Gamma distribution and those that follow the Gaussian distribution. To extract features from the two distinct distribution forms, a distributional convolutional autoencoder representation learning framework is proposed. By leveraging the information entropy of different data distributions, data distribution attention can incorporate distribution characteristics into representation learning features. Second, an HFF strategy enables a simple yet effective fusion of feature representations with different distributional characteristics. This strategy employs a multihead SA mechanism to assign weights to features from different distributions, thereby filtering out those that are beneficial for classification. Moreover, it allows for the further extraction of global information from PolSAR data. Finally, to make the feature fusion architecture better adapt to each stage of optimization, an H-CE loss function for combinatorial optimization is utilized. The experimental results on four standard datasets show that the proposed method has better classification results compared with the existing methods.
author: Jianlong Wang, Bingjie Zhang, Wenke Wang, Meijuan Yang, Siyun Hou, Junding Sun

title: SDFFNet: A Spatial-Frequency Domain Feature Fusion Network for Road Extraction From Very High-Resolution Satellite Images
content: Despite the progress of deep learning in road extraction from very high-resolution imagery, existing approaches often struggle with restricted receptive fields and insufficient global context modeling. These limitations become acute in complex urban environments, where shadows, occlusions, and interclass similarities lead to road discontinuities and localization errors. These limitations hinder their ability to handle complex urban scenarios characterized by large road-scale variations, high visual similarity between roads and surrounding land-cover classes, and severe occlusions caused by shadows, vegetation, and high-rise buildings, often resulting in fragmented and inaccurately localized road networks. We propose SDFFNet, a feature fusion network that combines a ResNet encoder with a strip convolution decoder to solve these challenges. It integrates a large kernel cross-dimensional attention submodule to reduce shadow occlusion effects and enhance spatial context modeling ability. A Fourier transform unit submodule captures global frequency domain information, while a spatial-frequency domain feature fusion module strengthens encoder–decoder interactions to mitigate interclass similarity interference. In addition, a spatial pixel fusion module aligns deep semantic information to minimize prediction errors caused by pixel positional deviations. Experimental results show that SDFFNet achieves Intersection over Union scores of 67.98%, 60.69%, and 64.85% on the Massachusetts, CHN6-CUG, and DeepGlobe datasets, respectively, outperforming comparable methods.
author: Danni Xue, Lin Bai, Wenqing Zhou, Yunqi Zhang, Amanda Gozho, SiWen Quan

title: PMDCFNet: A Progressive Multidomain Collaborative Fusion Network for Optical Image Cloud and Shadow Removal
content: Significant progress has been made in the reconstruction of cloud-free images using synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images as auxiliary information over the past few years. However, existing methods typically face the challenges of modal heterogeneity and insufficient available features due to cloud coverage and speckle noise. These methods only consider the spatial domain, which is not enough to achieve satisfactory results. To address the above issues, we propose a progressive multidomain feature fusion network that consists of four stages, named as PMDCFNet. To our knowledge, PMDCFNet combines the advantages of the gradient, spatial, and frequency domain to improve the performance. Specifically, we first design a multiscale gradient feature enhancement module in the feature extraction stage, which introduces the central difference convolution apart from traditional vanilla convolution to extract magnitude and gradient features simultaneously. Subsequently, the one-stage transformer is proposed to capture intermodal complementary and intramodal contextual relationships in the feature interaction stage, which integrates a sparse cross-attention and spatial-channel self-attention mechanisms into a unified network to reconstruct the features of cloud-contaminated regions. Then the frequency feature fusion module is established in the feature fusion stage, which combines the global characteristics of the Fourier transform with the local characteristics of the wavelet transform to fully fuse optical and SAR features in the frequency domain. Extensive experimental results confirm that PMDCFNet is much more competitive with eight state-of-the-art approaches in respect to the visual and objective evaluation results on the SEN12MS-CR and SMILE-CR datasets.
author: Zhongchen He, Tianman Sun, Mengxi Zhu, Peng Wang

title: YOLO-PDUOD: Pruning-Distillation With Multilevel Feature Aggregation for Underwater Object Detection
content: Underwater object detection is essential for autonomous underwater vehicles in marine exploration. However, the underwater environment is inherently complex due to light absorption and scattering effects. In practical scenarios, target objects often exhibit significant scale variation and are easily confused with complex backgrounds. These factors substantially increase detection difficulty. Meanwhile, limited onboard computational resources restrict the deployment of high-capacity models. To address these issues, this article proposes YOLO-PDUOD, a lightweight underwater object detection method based on YOLOv5. The multilevel feature aggregation mechanism integrating the split-channel fast extraction module and the information retention down-sampling module is designed to enhance feature representation across different semantic levels and preserve fine structural details. In addition, the texture perception module improves object–background discrimination. A joint lightweight optimization strategy combining channel pruning and knowledge distillation is further adopted to ensure computational efficiency while maintaining detection accuracy. Experiments on the URPC2020 dataset demonstrate that YOLO-PDUOD achieves an mAP@0.5 of 0.841 with only 1.87 M parameters and 4.6 giga floating-point operations, validating its effectiveness for real-time underwater applications.
author: Tingna Liu, Zongju Peng, Jiawei Xu, Fen Chen, Gangyi Jiang

title: A Dynamical Framework That Integrates Optical and SAR Imagery for Sugarcane Monitoring in the Cauca Valley
content: In this work, we present a novel dynamical framework for crop monitoring that integrates synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and optical satellite imagery. The proposed methodology employs 2-D state transitions to explicitly model the expected temporal evolution of crops. By combining such crop modeling with multisensor observations, including dual-polarimetric (VV and VH) radar images gathered by Sentinel-1 and optical imagery from Sentinel-2, the developed framework produces robust and consistent estimations using the well-established Kalman Filter. Specifically, the approach is applied to retrieve the biomass values of sugarcane crops located in the Cauca Valley (Colombia), over a full growing season (from summer 2024 to summer 2025). An intensive field campaign was carried out, so that ground-truth measurements in a large number of points, located in multiple fields, were available to develop and validate the proposed integration algorithm. Results show that biomass is accurately estimated especially when all available observations, both SAR and optical, are employed. In this case, a global root mean square error of 0.103 kg, a mean absolute error of 0.064 kg and an accumulated biomass difference of 13.8 kg$\cdot$day are obtained after evaluating the model multiple times on randomly selected points. The analysis also highlights a key limitation of optical imagery in the study area, as a large number of images are affected by cloud cover, resulting in long gaps within the time series and reduced estimation accuracy. Furthermore, the best performance of the proposed integrated model is achieved when all available SAR products, including dual-polarization (VV and VH) coherence and intensity products, are exploited, although very good results are also obtained using single-polarization coherence and intensity data (either at VV or VH polarizations). Comparison with alternative approaches, including partially integrated methods using only one type of information (either SAR or NDVI), and methods based solely on machine learning, indicates that the proposed methodology consistently offers superior accuracy in biomass estimation.
author: Alejandro Mestre-Quereda, Tomas Martinez-Marin, Juan M. Lopez-Sanchez, Carlos Mosquera, Henk Pelgrum, Antonia Skarli, Dirk Hoekman, Corné van-der Sande

title: Invariant Deformation Fields: Enabling Real-Time Visible–Infrared Image Alignment for UAVs
content: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly equipped with dual-modal sensors, making visible–infrared image alignment a task of significant practical value. However, precise cross-modal alignment remains a challenge due to disparities in field of view, radiation characteristics, and other factors. Existing image-based alignment methods often overlook the imaging mechanisms of dual-modal sensors and struggle to balance efficiency with accuracy. In this article, by investigating the imaging mechanism of a fixed visible–infrared sensor, we demonstrate that when its visible and infrared imaging planes are coplanar (a practical and feasible approximation), an ideal deformation field exists that can warp the visible image into alignment with the infrared image. This deformation field remains nearly invariant with respect to both UAV state (e.g., altitude, longitude, and latitude) and sensor pose (e.g., pitch, yaw, and roll). To accurately estimate this ideal deformation field, we propose PDFNet, an end-to-end pixelwise dense matching network. Unlike traditional methods that rely on sparse feature matching or parametric transformations, PDFNet directly learns a dense displacement field from visible to infrared images, enabling it to address complex geometric distortions and nonrigid deformations. In our alignment pipeline, images are first roughly aligned through manual annotation before being fed into the network. Then, by integrating complementary information from multiple rounds of manual annotation, the network effectively suppresses subjective errors and stably converges to a globally optimal deformation field that is invariant to input variations. This enables direct application of a single precomputed deformation field at inference, requiring no network forward pass and reaching 33 frames/s. Experiments on real-world UAV datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing learning-based methods such as RoMa and SuperGlue in alignment accuracy.
author: Zixuan Xiao, Ruigang Fu, Jiajun Xiang, Xingyue Liu, Pengcheng Wan, Ping Zhong

title: Self-SIDNet: A Self-Supervised Network for Winter Arctic Sea Ice Drift Extraction From AMSR2 Passive Microwave Data
content: Accurate monitoring of sea ice drift is essential for Arctic climate studies, navigation safety, and modeling polar ocean–atmosphere interactions. Passive microwave radiometers, such as Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer 2 (AMSR2), provide wide-swath, all-weather observations, making them valuable for drift extraction. However, existing template matching methods often produce sparse drift fields and rely on manually tuned thresholds, while conventional optical flow algorithms exhibit limited performance in terms of extraction accuracy. In this study, we propose a novel deep learning framework, Self-SIDNet, for extracting dense and accurate Arctic sea ice drift fields from AMSR2 89 GHz brightness temperature data. A cycle consistency loss is introduced as a self-supervision signal that enforces forward–backward drift coherence, which improves the performance of the method without relying on ground-truth labels. Self-SIDNet integrates photometric consistency, smoothness regularization, and cycle consistency into a unified loss function, and applies a sea ice concentration mask to restrict learning to ice-covered regions. Experiments show that Self-SIDNet achieves RMSEs of 6.17 and 6.75 cm/s for horizontal ($u$) and vertical ($v$) components, consistently outperforming traditional methods, including the traditional template-matching–based continuous maximum cross-correlation (CMCC) method and the traditional optical flow–based Farneback method. Notably, it provides full spatial coverage of the drift field, resolving the precision–coverage tradeoff in CMCC. Comparisons with the National Snow and Ice Data Center Polar Pathfinder product show high correlation and minimal bias, confirming the reliability of the extracted drift fields, while providing higher spatial resolution for fine-scale drift monitoring. These results demonstrate the potential of self-supervised deep learning for sea ice drift extraction from passive microwave data and suggest its applicability for large-scale, long-term monitoring in polar regions.
author: Peng Jiang, Tiantian Feng, Yushi Yang, Yuxuan Hu

title: Beyond Sequential Scanning: A Synergistic Mamba Paradigm With Adaptive Receptive Recalibration for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: State space models (SSMs) have emerged as a compelling paradigm for long-range dependence modeling with linear computational complexity. However, their intrinsic 1-D scanning process inevitably disrupts the local topological continuity of high-dimensional image structures. This limitation is particularly pronounced in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification, where existing Mamba-based methods frequently struggle with intricate spatial–spectral coupling and the deficiency of local inductive bias, resulting in suboptimal feature representation. To surmount these bottlenecks, we propose SynerMamba, a novel synergistic Mamba-based framework designed to reconstruct local structural integrity within the sequential modeling paradigm. Specifically, a spatial-spectral disentangler (SSD) is first introduced to explicitly decouple highly correlated spatial-spectral features via local dynamic graphs, establishing a structurally conscious manifold prior to SSM processing. To rectify the perception bias in sequential scanning, an adaptive receptive Mamba (ARM) is developed to inject multiscale spatial priors into the selective scanning process, effectively capturing fine-grained textures. Furthermore, a Mamba synergizer (MS) is engineered to orchestrate the sophisticated nonlinear interplay between spatial contexts and spectral signatures, transcending the representation constraints of conventional linear aggregation strategies. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SynerMamba achieves state-of-the-art classification performance while maintaining high computational efficiency.
author: Cheng Chen, Tao Wang, Jiping Cao, Qiying Wang, Yanzhao Su, Yao Ding

title: Enhanced Waterline Extraction From Airborne LiDAR Data Using Automated Density-Based Clustering
content: Monitoring shoreline evolution over time is important for assessing sediment transport around coastal areas, predicting erosion, and designing coastal protection strategies. Airborne light detection and ranging (LiDAR) provides high-resolution and elevation-rich data that can enable accurate shoreline monitoring under varying hydrodynamic conditions and with wide spatial coverage, compared to traditional ground-based shoreline monitoring methods. While recent advances in autonomous aerial vehicle (AAV) technology have expanded data accessibility, the extraction of shorelines from point cloud data remains constrained by the complex discrimination of land–water interfaces and a reliance on empirical parameter tuning. Consequently, efforts to develop standardized, physically interpretable extraction methods adaptable to diverse coastal settings remain limited. In this article, we built on previous works to develop a robust, automated algorithm for extracting statistical waterlines (SWL) from airborne LiDAR point clouds. We integrate a novel automated density clustering approach that systematically identifies optimal clustering parameter based on point cloud density distribution, removing the need for site-specific manual calibration. For development, AAV–LiDAR surveys were conducted to acquire point cloud datasets for a sandy beach in Singapore. The algorithm was optimized using four datasets from localized coastal sites along the beach and then validated using other datasets obtained from the surveys. Further validation was also conducted using the optimized parameters for two point-cloud datasets of Duders Beach, New Zealand, where the algorithm achieves submeter SWL extraction accuracy. Finally, a multitemporal case study at Balcomie Sands, Scotland, was carried out to demonstrate the algorithm’s applicability and consistency for monitoring intertidal zones under diverse coastal conditions. Long-term shoreline analysis, hazard assessment, and coastal management can now be performed by using the algorithm on high-resolution airborne LiDAR data.
author: Hieu Trung Kieu, Dawn Sok Cheng Pang, Adrian Wing-Keung Law

title: Time-Frequency Feature-Guided Progressive Attention DenseNet for Audio-Magnetotelluric Noise Detection
content: The audio-magnetotelluric (AMT) sounding method has been widely applied in mineral exploration and engineering geology. Unfortunately, field-observed signals are often contaminated by various random noises, causing the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) to change dynamically over time. Most existing time-domain denoising methods process the entire observation sequence uniformly and do not adaptively distinguish signals according to temporal variations in signal quality. However, certain time segments inherently possess high SNR and, thus, require no additional denoising. Uniform processing not only increases computational cost but may also overprocess useful signals. To address this issue, we propose a time-frequency feature-guided progressive attention-dense network (TF-PADenseNet) for AMT noise detection. The method first employs the continuous wavelet transform to convert time-domain signals into time-frequency feature maps within a unified representation space, thereby revealing the energy distribution characteristics across both time and frequency. Next, a progressive attention-dense network is constructed by integrating squeeze-and-excitation and convolutional block attention module mechanisms to progressively refine and reconstruct feature weights, enabling the network to distinguish between high-quality and noisy signals. Finally, a classification layer produces the recognition results, achieving precise identification of noisy and useful signals across different time segments. Simulation results show that the proposed TF-PADenseNet achieves an accuracy of 98.01% on the test dataset, outperforming several mainstream noise detection methods. Field experiments further demonstrate that TF-PADenseNet exhibits strong robustness under complex electromagnetic conditions. When combined with any denoising algorithm, its detection results can further improve data quality, yielding apparent resistivity and phase curves that are both smooth and continuous while preserving key signal features.
author: Dian Hong, Jin Li, Jiayu Wang, Qunbin Qi, Jianmin Li, Jingtian Tang

title: A Survey on SAR Ship Classification Using Deep Learning
content: Deep learning (DL) has become a central approach for ship classification using synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery. This survey reviews 74 representative studies selected from 187 publications, categorizing them into a taxonomy with four main dimensions: 1) DL architectures; 2) datasets; 3) image augmentation; and 4) learning techniques. We analyze how approaches such as handcrafted feature integration, data augmentation, fine-tuning, and transfer learning influence classification performance, and summarize the use of public benchmarks including OpenSARShip and FUSARShip. This survey highlights key challenges: limited data availability, class imbalance, lack of standardized metrics, and limited interpretability of DL models. Future research directions include the development of SAR-specific DL architectures, advanced augmentation and generative approaches, integration of handcrafted and deep features, interpretable DL, and stronger interdisciplinary collaboration. By addressing these challenges, DL-based SAR ship classification can achieve greater robustness, accuracy, and transparency, ultimately strengthening maritime surveillance and operational monitoring.
author: Ch Muhammad Awais, Marco Reggiannini, Davide Moroni, Emanuele Salerno

title: Fully Automatic Landing Site Selection by a UAV in an Unknown Environment
content: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are widely deployed due to their low cost, autonomy, flexibility, and ability to operate in hard-to-reach areas. However, autonomous landing in unknown environments in the presence of hazards such as water or rough terrain remains challenging. In this article, we propose a multicriteria decision-making framework for safe landing site selection based on colored three-dimensional (3-D) point clouds, from which parameters such as local slope, local roughness, distance from the UAV, and proximity to hazards are extracted. These parameters are combined using a fuzzy inference system to calculate an overall safety index, the highest value of which indicates the safest landing location. To estimate slope and roughness, we also propose a method based on anisotropic diffusion filtering for decorrelating these parameters while preserving topographic discontinuities. Validation of this method on 50 synthetic digital elevation models yielded high accuracy, with a slope root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.45° and a PCC of 99.76%, and a roughness RMSE of 0.08 m and a PCC of 58.66%, thereby outperforming conventional methods. We also enhanced the performance of semantic segmentation for water body detection using superpixelization. An accuracy of 97.99% and an F1 Score of 79.14% are achieved, thereby ensuring reliable identification of this hazard. The overall framework was tested on a semi-synthetic scene featuring a water surface and two real 3-D scenes obtained from a UAV and containing varied terrain, including sand dunes, gravel, vegetation, and rocky slopes. The safest landing site was consistently selected for all UAV positions and altitudes, with total processing times ranging from 8.1 to 281 s, demonstrating near real-time feasibility.
author: Salim Iratni, Moussa Diaf

title: Spatiotemporal Deformation Monitoring and Failure-Runout Process Prediction of the Xieliupo Landslide
content: The Bailong River Basin is characterized by active tectonics and numerous creeping landslides, with the Xieliupo landslide posing significant potential river-blocking hazards. Conventional interferometric synthetic aperture radar methods are often limited by decorrelation and large deformation gradients in steep terrain. This study utilizes Lutan-1 L-band synthetic aperture radar data using pixel offset tracking and time-series analysis to retrieve the four-dimensional deformation characteristics of the landslide, complemented and cross-validated by high-precision point observations from an artificial corner reflector (CR). Based on deformation partitioning and kinematic interpretation, two representative source-area scenarios were designed for numerical simulations to assess runout pathways and river-blocking risks under extreme conditions. Results indicate that the landslide persistently moves northwestward with marked spatial heterogeneity, exhibiting a partitioned kinematic structure characterized by rear thrust loading, central restraint, lower tensile decoupling, and frontal obstruction. CR monitoring reveals a step-like evolution with alternating acceleration and deceleration. Spring acceleration coincides with the transition of air temperatures from below-zero to above-zero, suggesting that freeze–thaw and snowmelt processes likely play an important role, while rainfall-deformation responses exhibit significant decoupling across stages. Numerical simulations demonstrate that hazard magnitude is significantly constrained by the source-area extent: failure restricted to the rear zone primarily deposits on the central platform, whereas failure involving the middle-to-lower zone may generate rapid long-runout movement and river blockage. These findings provide a useful reference for integrating remote sensing-derived deformation zoning with scenario-based landslide hazard assessment.
author: Hezhen Liu, Yongsheng Li, Pengqing Shi, Xiaolong Zhou, Bin Liu, Bingquan Li, Qiang Li

title: An Edge-Guided Structural Transformer Network for SAR Time-Series Land Cover Mapping
content: Real-time and accurate interpretation of time-series synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is vital for land-cover monitoring. Nevertheless, the presence of speckle noise makes it extremely challenging to distill the essential information from redundant SAR time series. Current methods not only struggle to suppress temporal-feature redundancy but also tend to overlook the structural cues along land-cover boundaries that are crucial for boosting classification performance, leading to slower inference and suboptimal accuracy. To address these challenges, we propose edge-guided structural transformer network (EGSTNet), a lightweight and efficient land cover mapping network for time-series SAR imagery. The edge-guided convolution and axial separable large kernel attention, based on a lightweight design, utilize a reparameterizable multibranch convolutional module and a large-kernel attention mechanism to capture multiorder gradient features and model global spatiotemporal characteristics along both spatial and temporal dimensions in SAR time series. Notably, an implicit semantic graph style is incorporated into implicit semantic graph transformer to facilitate efficient joint modeling of spatiotemporal and structural information of land covers. Experimental results on three benchmark time-series SAR land cover mapping datasets show that EGSTNet achieves mean intersection over union scores of 35.87%, 48.16%, and 50.27% on the Slovenia, Brandenburg S1, and Brandenburg S2 datasets, respectively, with 17.10 M trainable parameters and an inference speed of 105.53 frames per second.
author: Kang Ni, Jiaxing Ren, Chunyang Yuan, Zhizhong Zheng, Peng Wang

title: Matching-Based Extraction of Independent Ice Floe Motion in the Arctic Marginal Ice Zone
content: The extraction of independent ice floe motion in the Arctic marginal ice zone (MIZ) plays a crucial role in motion prediction, polar climate research, and ensuring navigation safety. In this study, we propose an integrated algorithm framework that combines ice floe contour masking with the A-KAZE feature tracking method to extract high-precision motion data from time-series MODIS imagery. The algorithm’s performance was evaluated across five subregions. Comparisons with the National Snow and Ice Data Center sea-ice drift product indicated high accuracy and stability. In addition, results aligned closely with the classical linear free-drift model, demonstrating that the algorithm successfully captures the actual motion trends of ice floes driven by wind and ocean currents. Further validation against manually identified data revealed strong consistency in both ice floe centroid positions and motion vectors. These findings underscore the method’s robustness and reliability, making it a valuable tool for understanding and predicting ice floe dynamics in the MIZ.
author: Rui Zhang, Adan Wu, Jinlei Chen, Qingchao Xu, Liuhao Wang, Kaihui Zhang, Yilei Xiao, Lei Fu, Fuhong Deng, Rui Wang

title: MDDS-Net: Dual-Backbone Network With Dynamic Enhancement for SAR Ship Detection in Complex Maritime Environments
content: Ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images is a key technology for maritime surveillance. However, practical applications face severe physical challenges, including low signal-to-clutter and signal-to-noise ratios under high sea states, discontinuous scattering of weak targets, and multitarget occlusion in dense ports. Existing methods struggle to suppress these severe interferences while preserving weak target structures. To address these issues, this article proposes a detection framework based on a dual-backbone complementary coordination mechanism. The method employs a parallel-designed local and global encoder to extract fine-grained spatial details of ships and model long-range scene dependencies, respectively. To explicitly tackle the aforementioned physical degradations, two core modules are introduced. First, a geometric prior-guided multiscale feature enhancement module integrates ship shape priors into spatial attention to aggregate discrete scattering centers, addressing weak target discontinuity and occlusion. Second, a bidirectional gated feature fusion module enables adaptive cross-backbone feature decoupling to suppress severe sea clutter in low SNR/SCR environments. In addition, an adaptive tiling strategy guided by land-sea masks is employed to efficiently process large-scale SAR images. Experiments demonstrate that multiscale dual-branch dependency synergy network achieves superior performance on the HRSID and RSDD-SAR datasets. Cross-validation using GF-3 satellite data and automatic identification system data further validates the robustness and practical value of our approach under complex maritime conditions.
author: Weimin Kong, Yun Zhang, Long Li, Niezipeng Kang, Qinglong Hua

title: Frequency-Domain Mixing and Boundary Modulation Network for SAR Small Target Detection
content: As synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging resolution gradually improves to the submeter level, the fine scattering structures of both targets and background clutter in the scene are simultaneously restored with high fidelity. The degree of feature aliasing between small targets (such as small ships) and speckle noise or nearshore clutter is significantly intensified, and the difficulty of stable feature extraction is greatly increased. On the one hand, traditional multiscale methods (i.e., feature pyramids and fixed-scale convolutions) use discrete hierarchical designs, which fail to adapt to the continuous-scale variations of SAR small objects and lead to inefficient feature extraction. On the other hand, SAR small objects have significant local radar scattering differences; even different regions of the same ship show drastic feature changes, which severely reduces intraclass feature similarity and consistency. To address these issues, this article proposes an SAR ship detection method based on frequency-domain mixing and part-aware representation. First, a frequency-domain differential decoupling module is designed, which realizes adaptive frequency band division through multiscale Gaussian filtering and filters key ship frequency features combined with a dynamic masking mechanism. This module can not only suppress speckle noise but also construct a continuous-scale feature mapping space. Second, a part-aware dynamic convolution module is developed to fuse local morphological features and global contextual information for generating dynamic dilation rate convolution kernels, so as to accurately depict target boundary contours in dense scenes and suppress feature aliasing between targets. Comparative experiments on two mainstream datasets, i.e., HRSID and MSAR-1.0, demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of small target detection, dense scene adaptability, and robustness to strong noise.
author: Wenbin Gao, Zhi Yuan, Donglin Jing

title: Corrections to “3-D Reconstruction of Ship Target From Multiangle SAR Images Based on Side-View Projection”
content: In the above article [1], Fig. 26 is incorrect. The corrected figure is given here. Note that the bottommost part of the 3-D reconstruction result corresponds to the deck area illuminated by the SAR signal, and its width is considered to represent the true width of the ship. Therefore, the measured width in [1] is the width at the very bottom of the O-XZ plane of the 3-D reconstruction result.
author: Jindeng Li, Xiangguang Leng, Yifan Liu, Zheng Zhou, Kefeng Ji

title: SPCMap: A Power-Aware and Compilation-Efficient Mapping Framework for Heterogeneous CGRAs in Onboard SAR Imaging
content: Onboard real-time synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging is essential for time-sensitive applications but faces strict power constraints on satellites. Heterogeneous coarse-grained reconfigurable arrays (CGRAs) with circuit-switched networks offer an efficient and flexible platform for SAR computing, yet existing mapping methods fail to jointly optimize power and compilation time efficiency. This article introduces SAR-aware Power-and-Compilation-Efficient Mapping (SPCMap), a SAR-aware mapping framework for heterogeneous CGRAs that tackles this joint challenge. SPCMap features four innovations. 1) a power-aware modulo routing resource graph modeling method for accurate power estimation. 2) An anchor diffusion method leveraging the multisource convergence pattern of SAR data flows to prune the search space. 3) a nongreedy progressive search method to escape local optima. 4) A parameterized active backtracking method for robustness. Evaluated on 13 real SAR data flow graphs (DFGs) using a 12-nm CGRA platform, SPCMap achieves 100% mapping success. It reduces router power by up to 62.7%, with an average deviation of only 2.4% from the theoretical optimum, while keeping compilation time within 1.4× of the fastest greedy baseline. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component. In cross-platform comparisons, the proposed CGRA with SPCMap attains a leading normalized energy efficiency of 3.44×107 pixel2/s/W, outperforming existing processors. SPCMap thus balances power, compilation time, and reliability, providing a practical compilation solution for energy-efficient onboard SAR imaging.
author: Yongrui Li, Liang Chen, Han Li, Hao Shi, Yizhuang Xie, Wang Chao, Wei Li, Linlin Ge

title: Dynamic Hypergraph-Guided Mamba for TLS Point Cloud Foliage Separation
content: Effective foliage-wood separation plays a crucial role in forestry applications, such as leaf area index (LAI) estimation and quantitative structure model (QSM). Point clouds provide valuable support for this task. However, large-scale scenes, uneven density, and occlusions hinder the use of general 3-D vision methods. Existing transformer-based methods typically partition point clouds into local patches, but the high computational complexity restricts the feasible patch size, often fragmenting tree structures and causing semantic information loss. Moreover, the geometric similarity of fine-scale foliage, coupled with limited context in small patches, makes feature discrimination more difficult. These two issues severely limit the performance of existing methods on foliage segmentation tasks. To address these challenges, we propose the dynamic hypergraph-guided Mamba (DHMamba) model with two key innovations. First, we leverage a lightweight Mamba-based architecture whose linear complexity enables processing of larger patches, thereby expanding the receptive field and reducing erroneous segmentation of branches and leaves. Second, we introduce a dynamic hypergraph-based serialization strategy to capture higher order topological dependencies within local regions, enhancing the model’s ability to extract discriminative features. Moreover, by introducing prior global density and designing two geometric feature descriptors, planarity and linearity, our framework further enhances the multiscale discrimination of subtle differences in canopy. Extensive experiments on individual-tree and plot-scale datasets demonstrate that DHMamba substantially advances segmentation accuracy and robustness, highlighting its strong potential for practical large-scale forest point-cloud analysis and sustainable forest-resource management.
author: Weipeng Jing, Wenjun Zhang, Xuancheng Zhang, Donglin Di, Ajmal Mian, Muhammad Ibrahim, Chao Li

title: Fusion of Multisource Data for Comprehensive Assessment of Phytoplankton Composition
content: Understanding phytoplankton composition and distribution is essential for assessing marine ecosystem processes, yet satellite and in situ datasets each have substantial limitations. This study develops a fusion framework that combines gap-filled satellite-derived total chlorophyll a (TChla) and phytoplankton functional type (PFT) chlorophyll a (Chla) concentrations with in situ data points extracted from a hyperspectral flow-through spectrophotometric system (ACS). The objective is to generate spatially continuous and accurate Chla fields. The in situ hyperspectral absorption data obtained during the RV Polarstern PS113 expedition were used to derive TChla and PFT Chla concentrations with associated uncertainties, using an empirical orthogonal function based regression model. The ACS-derived estimates were then merged with the gap-filled satellite products through optimal interpolation to produce a consistent spatiotemporal dataset. The ACS-derived TChla and PFT Chla products showed strong agreement with the ground truth Chla concentrations (HPLC-Chla), with the coefficient of determination (R2) values above 0.66 for all groups and median-percentage-deviation (MedPD) values between 27% and 46%. The fusion dataset further improved performance, reaching R2 values higher than 0.69 and MedPD values between 21% and 44%. The combined product provides spatially and temporally continuous Chla fields and resolves regions with extensive satellite data gaps more effectively than either dataset alone. This fusion approach supports more reliable analyses of phytoplankton dynamics and can guide sampling strategies in areas with limited satellite coverage. Future work should focus on refining uncertainty estimates and testing the framework across different oceanic regions to improve its stability and broader applicability.
author: Ehsan Mehdipour, Hongyan Xi, Alexander Barth, Adalbert Wilhelm, Astrid Bracher

title: Nine-Year Observation of Global Precipitation Particle Size Distribution and Radar Reflectivity
content: Understanding global precipitation microphysics is essential for quantitative precipitation estimation (QPE) and numerical weather prediction (NWP). Using 9 years (2015–2023) of the global precipitation measurement mission Dual-frequency Precipitation Radar (DPR) observations over the quasi-global domain (67 °S–67 °N), we characterize precipitation particle size distribution (PSD) properties for rainfall and snowfall. We analyze the mass-weighted mean diameter (${{D}_m}$), normalized intercept parameter (${{N}_w}$), radar reflectivity ($Z$), and dual-frequency ratio ($\mathrm{DFR}$), stratified by land/ocean, convective/stratiform type, altitude, and phase. Rainfall rates are grouped into six classes and snowfall rates into five classes to quantify intensity dependence. To interpret DPR dual-frequency signatures, we develop an observation-constrained forward-scattering framework that adopts DPR-consistent PSD and particle-shape assumptions and performs T-matrix simulations for rain, dry snow, and wet snow with varying liquid–water volume fraction (${{F}_{vw}}$), evaluated against 9-year near-surface $\mathrm{DFR}$-$Z$ statistics. Quasi-global PSD peaks (${{D}_m}$∼1.0 mm; log10${{N}_w}$∼3.4) closely resemble oceanic precipitation, and convective and stratiform regimes substantially overlap in ${{D}_m}$ (0.9–1.8 mm) and log10${{N}_w}$ (3.0–4.0). Vertically, ${{D}_m}$ skewness and kurtosis decrease from 2–6 km, with distinct features near ∼10 and ∼15 km. Rainfall drop size distributions become more variable at high intensities (>5 mm h−1), whereas snowfall PSDs remain comparatively stable (${{D}_m}$ mainly 1.0–1.5 mm; log10${{N}_w}$∼3.4). Forward-scattering comparisons show that dry-snow assumptions cannot reproduce the observed $\mathrm{DFR}$-$Z$ space, while increasing ${{F}_{vw}}$ yields close agreement, indicating that near-surface snowfall is consistent with wetted melted particles. These results provide observation-based constraints for improving solid-hydrometeor scattering representations in DPR snowfall retrievals and microphysical parameterizations in NWP.
author: Qingpeng Wu, Wei Cheng, V. Chandrasekar, Tiantian Yu, Shuangshuang Bian, Hao Wang, Qiangyu Zeng

title: Hyperbolic Geometry-Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation in Remote Sensing
content: Unsupervised domain adaptive semantic segmentation (UDA-SS) for remote sensing imagery remains challenging due to the substantial distribution shifts across regions and sensors. Existing teacher–student methods mainly rely on pixel-level consistency and high-confidence pseudolabel fitting in Euclidean space. However, these strategies provide only local supervision and fail to preserve the global geometric structure of semantic classes, leading to cluster drift, weak class discrimination, and error accumulation in challenging regions, such as object boundaries and fine-grained structures. To address these issues, we propose HyperDASeg, a hyperbolic geometry-guided framework for UDA-SS in remote sensing. Specifically, HyperDASeg adopts a two-stage adaptation paradigm on the Poincaré ball, transforming hyperbolic geometry from an auxiliary embedding choice into an explicit adaptation prior that jointly governs representation shaping and self-training. In the first stage, hyperbolic prototype distillation (HPD) leverages a vision foundation model as the teacher and performs prototype-based structured distillation in hyperbolic space, encouraging compact intraclass clustering and clear interclass separation. In the second stage, we introduce a hyperbolic radius uncertainty-weighted self-training strategy, which uses the hyperbolic radius to estimate pixel uncertainty and progressively emphasizes hard target regions that are often overlooked by conventional high-confidence-first learning. Extensive experiments conducted on the ISPRS and LoveDA datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. On the ISPRS dataset, HyperDASeg outperforms previous state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods by an average of 3.53% in terms of mean intersection over union (mIoU) across four adaptation tasks; on the LoveDA dataset, it outperforms previous SOTA methods by 1.32% in the mIoU value.
author: Jiaxin Yan, Wenzhe Liu, Hongfu Li, Jing Zhang, Qianqi Lu, Yanming Guo, Yuxiang Xie

title: Can Meter-Scale Optical Imagery Enable Species-Level Vegetation Monitoring in Hydrologically Dynamic Riparian Zones?
content: Monitoring species-level vegetation dynamics within mountainous riparian zones is challenging due to hydrological variability, intricate terrain, and fragmented vegetation corridors, which amplify mixed-pixel effects. Consequently, sub-meter imagery is conventionally deemed necessary, yet high costs and limited availability constrain continuous monitoring under such dynamic conditions. This study evaluates the feasibility of using meter-scale satellite imagery (PlanetScope) for species-level monitoring in these dynamic environments. Specifically, we investigate whether dominant species coverage (CDS) can be accurately mapped by combining multiple machine learning models. To mitigate the pronounced temporal variability driven by fluctuating water levels, we further assess the efficacy of a multi-temporal integration (MTI) strategy to stabilize these species-level vegetation signals. Results indicate that single-date predictions are highly sensitive to acquisition timing, with R2 varying from 0.53 to 0.90 due to meteorological conditions, water-level fluctuations, and phenology. In contrast, the MTI strategy markedly enhanced prediction accuracy and temporal consistency by integrating multi-date predictions. For the best-performing model, MTI increased the R2 to 0.91 and reduced the RMSE to 0.09, with initially lower-performing models exhibiting greater relative improvements after integration. Overall, the results demonstrate that meter-scale satellite imagery, when integrated with frequent temporal sampling, can yield reliable species-level data in narrow and heterogeneous riparian environments, which have traditionally been thought to necessitate sub-meter resolution. This underscores the practical applicability of meter-scale data for ecological monitoring in complex riparian systems.
author: Xu Zhou, Shengjun Wu, Yutao Gao, Zhaofei Wen

title: A Diffusion Model With Efficient Sampling and Prior-Guidance for Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution
content: Diffusion models have recently achieved rapid progress in super-resolution (SR). Nevertheless, for remote sensing image super-resolution (RSISR), existing diffusion-based methods remain limited by two bottlenecks. First, high-quality reconstruction typically relies on a relatively large number of sampling steps, which leads to low sampling efficiency. Second, most methods condition only on the low-resolution (LR) image, providing limited prior information and thereby constraining reconstruction performance. To address these bottlenecks, we propose a novel diffusion model for RSISR, termed EPGDiffSR, which combines efficient sampling with prior guidance. First, we design a new linear–exponential hybrid easing sampling strategy that preserves fine detail reconstruction with fewer sampling steps. Second, we propose structural prior injection (SPI) and prior-guided sampling (PGS) to consistently incorporate prior information throughout the diffusion process. Specifically, SPI introduces a structure-aware, pixel-wise bias term into the noise injection step during the forward diffusion process, whereas PGS corrects the predicted noise in the reverse generation process by constructing a regularization term and encoding LR image features to guide the denoising. Experimental results demonstrate that on both Potsdam and Toronto datasets, our method EPGDiffSR consistently outperforms representative state-of-the-art methods across both 4× and 8× SR tasks in terms of perceptual metrics, including learned perceptual image patch similarity and Fréchet inception distance. Furthermore, under real-world SR evaluation on the UC Merced dataset, EPGDiffSR is able to reconstruct images with improved visual quality.
author: Zhongmei Wang, Gaoxuan Wu, Ying Zhang, Jianhua Liu

title: ISAR Technique for Inland Waterway Surveillance Using Spaceborne Single-Channel SAR Data
content: This article proposes an inverse synthetic aperture radar technique specifically tailored for inland waterway surveillance using single-channel spaceborne SAR data. The approach delivers well-focused vessel images on a homogeneous plane, enabling reliable size estimation and supporting automatic target recognition. In addition, it jointly estimates vessel kinematic parameters, including speed and heading, and corrects azimuthal displacement to recover the actual vessel position. A key novelty lies in exploiting the elongated structural characteristics of ships for their positioning without relying on along-track interferometry, multiplatform acquisitions, or wake detection. Comprehensive theoretical and numerical performance analyses are presented under different use cases. The proposed method is further validated using real SAR data acquired over different waterways and vessel types, demonstrating robust performance and practical applicability across a wide range of operational scenarios relevant to inland traffic.
author: Ilaria Nasso, Fabrizio Santi, Debora Pastina
